title: Hungarian Revolution of 1956

infobox.value: * Until 28 October: * [Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic") * From 4 November: * [Kádár government](/wiki/Revolutionary_Workers%27-Peasants%27_Government_of_Hungary "Revolutionary Workers'-Peasants' Government of Hungary") | * **Hungarian revolutionaries** * From 28 October: * [Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic") ([Nagy government](/wiki/Governments_of_Imre_Nagy "Governments of Imre Nagy")) |

* [Yuri Andropov](/wiki/Yuri_Andropov "Yuri Andropov") * [Ivan Konev](/wiki/Ivan_Konev "Ivan Konev") * [Ivan Serov](/wiki/Ivan_Serov "Ivan Serov") * [Georgy Zhukov](/wiki/Georgy_Zhukov "Georgy Zhukov") * [Kuzma Grebennik](/wiki/Kuzma_Grebennik "Kuzma Grebennik") * [Gennady Obaturov](/wiki/Gennady_Obaturov "Gennady Obaturov") * [Vasily Sokolovsky](/wiki/Vasily_Sokolovsky "Vasily Sokolovsky") * **[Ernő Gerő](/wiki/Ern%C5%91_Ger%C5%91 "Ernő Gerő")** * [András Hegedüs](/wiki/Andr%C3%A1s_Heged%C3%BCs "András Hegedüs") * [László Piros](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Piros "László Piros") * [István Bata](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Bata "István Bata") * **[János Kádár](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_K%C3%A1d%C3%A1r "János Kádár")** * [Ferenc Münnich](/wiki/Ferenc_M%C3%BCnnich "Ferenc Münnich") * [Antal Apró](/wiki/Antal_Apr%C3%B3 "Antal Apró") | * **[Imre Nagy](/wiki/Imre_Nagy "Imre Nagy")** [Executed](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Executed") * [Pál Maléter](/wiki/P%C3%A1l_Mal%C3%A9ter "Pál Maléter") [Executed](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Executed") * [Miklós Gimes](/wiki/Mikl%C3%B3s_Gimes "Miklós Gimes") [Executed](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Executed") * [Géza Losonczy](/wiki/G%C3%A9za_Losonczy "Géza Losonczy") * [Zoltán Tildy](/wiki/Zolt%C3%A1n_Tildy "Zoltán Tildy") * [Béla Kovács](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Kov%C3%A1cs_(politician,_1908) "Béla Kovács (politician, 1908)") * [Anna Kéthly](/wiki/Anna_K%C3%A9thly "Anna Kéthly") * [József Dudás](/wiki/J%C3%B3zsef_Dud%C3%A1s "József Dudás")  [Executed](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Executed") * [Sándor Kopácsi](/wiki/S%C3%A1ndor_Kop%C3%A1csi "Sándor Kopácsi") ([POW](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war")) * [Béla Király](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Kir%C3%A1ly "Béla Király") * [Gergely Pongrátz](/wiki/Gergely_Pongr%C3%A1tz "Gergely Pongrátz") |

(23 October – 4 November 1956; [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *1956-os forradalom*), also known as the **Hungarian Uprising**, was an attempted countrywide [revolution](/wiki/Revolution "Revolution") against the government of the [Hungarian People's Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic") (1949–1989) and the policies caused by the government's subordination to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (USSR).[[nb 2]](#cite_note-5) The uprising lasted 15 days before being crushed by Soviet tanks and troops on 7 November 1956 (outside of Budapest firefights lasted until at least 12 November 1956).[[4]](#cite_note-Granville-6) Thousands were killed or wounded, and nearly a quarter of a million Hungarians fled the country.[[5]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-8)

The **Hungarian Revolution of 1956** (23 October – 4 November 1956; [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *1956-os forradalom*), also known as the **Hungarian Uprising**, was an attempted countrywide [revolution](/wiki/Revolution "Revolution") against the government of the [Hungarian People's Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic") (1949–1989) and the policies caused by the government's subordination to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (USSR).[[nb 2]](#cite_note-5) The uprising lasted 15 days before being crushed by Soviet tanks and troops on 7 November 1956 (outside of Budapest firefights lasted until at least 12 November 1956).[[4]](#cite_note-Granville-6) Thousands were killed or wounded, and nearly a quarter of a million Hungarians fled the country.[[5]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-8)

The Hungarian Revolution began on 23 October 1956 in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest") when [university students](/wiki/Student "Student") appealed to the civil populace to join them at the [Hungarian Parliament Building](/wiki/Hungarian_Parliament_Building "Hungarian Parliament Building") to protest against the USSR's geopolitical domination of Hungary through the [Stalinist](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") government of [Mátyás Rákosi](/wiki/M%C3%A1ty%C3%A1s_R%C3%A1kosi "Mátyás Rákosi"). A delegation of students entered the building of [Magyar Rádió](/wiki/Magyar_R%C3%A1di%C3%B3 "Magyar Rádió") to broadcast their [sixteen demands](/wiki/Demands_of_Hungarian_Revolutionaries_of_1956 "Demands of Hungarian Revolutionaries of 1956") for political and economic reforms to civil society, but were detained by security guards. When the student protestors outside the radio building demanded the release of their delegation, a group of police from the ÁVH ([State Protection Authority](/wiki/State_Protection_Authority "State Protection Authority")) fatally shot several of the students.[[7]](#cite_note-9) The siege of the Radio building began shortly after midnight and ended around 8 a.m. At that point, the revolutionaries broke into the building and treated the soldiers, officer cadets, and members of the ÁVH (State Protection Authority) found there as prisoners. ÁVH officer Major Fehér was executed with a shot to the back of the head.

Consequently, Hungarians organized into revolutionary [militias](/wiki/Militia "Militia") to fight against the ÁVH; local Hungarian communist leaders and ÁVH policemen were captured and summarily executed; and political prisoners were released and armed. To realize their political, economic, and social demands, local [soviets](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") (councils of workers) assumed control of municipal government from the [Hungarian Working People's Party](/wiki/Hungarian_Working_People%27s_Party "Hungarian Working People's Party") (*Magyar Dolgozók Pártja*). The new government of [Imre Nagy](/wiki/Imre_Nagy "Imre Nagy") disbanded the ÁVH (on 28 October), declared Hungary's withdrawal from the [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") (on 1 November), and pledged to re-establish free elections. By the end of October the intense fighting had subsided.

Although initially showing willingness to negotiate the withdrawal of the [Soviet Army](/wiki/Soviet_Army "Soviet Army") from Hungary (having negotiations on 3 November), the USSR repressed the Hungarian Revolution on 4 November 1956, and fought the Hungarian revolutionaries until Soviet victory on 10 November; repression of the Hungarian Uprising killed 2,500 Hungarians and 700 Soviet Army soldiers, and compelled 200,000 Hungarians to seek political refuge abroad, mostly to [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria").[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Second World War")]

During the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") (1939–1945), the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") (1920–1946) was a member of the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") – in alliance with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy"), the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"), and the [Kingdom of Bulgaria](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Bulgaria_(1908%E2%80%931946) "Tsardom of Bulgaria (1908–1946)"). In 1941, the [Royal Hungarian Army](/wiki/Hungary_in_World_War_II "Hungary in World War II") participated in the Nazi [invasion of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") (6 April 1941) and in [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") (22 June 1941), the invasion of the USSR. In the event, by 1944, the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") were on the way to the Kingdom of Hungary, after first having repelled the Royal Hungarian Army and the armies of the other Axis Powers from the territory of the USSR.[[10]](#cite_note-brittannica-12)

Fearful of the Red Army's occupation of the Kingdom of Hungary, the royal Hungarian government unsuccessfully sought an armistice with the Allies, which was a betrayal of the Axis powers. The Germans launched [Operation Margarethe](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Hungary_(1944) "German invasion of Hungary (1944)") (12 March 1944) to establish the Nazi [Government of National Unity](/wiki/Government_of_National_Unity_(Hungary) "Government of National Unity (Hungary)") of Hungary; the short-lived puppet government existed for less than a year and Hungary was occupied by the Soviet Union in 1945 after the [Siege of Budapest](/wiki/Siege_of_Budapest "Siege of Budapest").[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[10]](#cite_note-brittannica-12)

At the end of the Second World War (1939–1945), the Kingdom of Hungary was in the geopolitical [sphere of influence](/wiki/Sphere_of_influence "Sphere of influence") of the USSR. In the political aftermath of the War, Hungary was a [multi-party](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"), in which the [1945 Hungarian parliamentary election](/wiki/1945_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "1945 Hungarian parliamentary election") produced a coalition government composed of [Independent Smallholders, Agrarian Workers and the Civic Party](/wiki/Independent_Smallholders,_Agrarian_Workers_and_Civic_Party "Independent Smallholders, Agrarian Workers and Civic Party"), headed by President [Zoltán Tildy](/wiki/Zolt%C3%A1n_Tildy "Zoltán Tildy") and Prime Minister [Ferenc Nagy](/wiki/Ferenc_Nagy "Ferenc Nagy"). Nonetheless, on behalf of the USSR, the [Hungarian Communist Party](/wiki/Hungarian_Communist_Party "Hungarian Communist Party") continually used [salami slicing tactics](/wiki/Salami_slicing_tactics "Salami slicing tactics") to wrest minor political concessions, which continually diminished the political authority of the coalition government – despite the Communist Party only having received 17 percent of the votes in the parliamentary election of 1945.[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-UNPara47-15)

After the election in 1945, control of the [State Protection Authority](/wiki/State_Protection_Authority "State Protection Authority") (*Államvédelmi Hatóság*, ÁVH) was transferred from the Independent Smallholders Party of the coalition government to the Hungarian Communist party.[[14]](#cite_note-16) The ÁVH repressed non‑communist political opponents with [intimidation](/wiki/Intimidation "Intimidation") and false accusations, imprisonment and [torture](/wiki/Torture "Torture").[[15]](#cite_note-17) The brief, four‑year period of multi-party democracy ended when the [Social Democratic Party of Hungary](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Hungary "Social Democratic Party of Hungary") merged with the Communist Party and became the [Hungarian Working People's Party](/wiki/Hungarian_Working_People%27s_Party "Hungarian Working People's Party"), whose candidate stood unopposed in the [1949 Hungarian parliamentary election](/wiki/1949_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "1949 Hungarian parliamentary election"). Afterwards, on 20 August 1949, the [Hungarian People's Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic") was proclaimed and established as a socialist state, with whom the USSR then concluded the [COMECON](/wiki/Comecon "Comecon") treaty of mutual assistance, which allowed stationing troops of Red Army soldiers in Hungary.[[13]](#cite_note-UNPara47-15)

Based upon the economic model of the USSR, the Hungarian Working People's Party established the [socialist economy](/wiki/Socialist_economics "Socialist economics") of Hungary with the [nationalization](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") and of the natural resources of the country. Moreover, by 1955, the relatively free politics of Hungary allowed [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual") and journalists to freely criticise the social and economic policies of the Rákosi government.[[16]](#cite_note-18) In that vein of relative political freedom, on 22 October 1956, students from the [Budapest University of Technology and Economics](/wiki/Budapest_University_of_Technology_and_Economics "Budapest University of Technology and Economics") had reestablished the MEFESZ [students' union](/wiki/Students%27_union "Students' union"), which the Rákosi government earlier had banned for their politically incorrect politics.[[17]](#cite_note-Crampton295-19)

### Stalinist Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stalinist Hungary")]

Initially, the Hungarian People's Republic was a [socialist](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") state headed by the Communist government of [Mátyás Rákosi](/wiki/M%C3%A1ty%C3%A1s_R%C3%A1kosi "Mátyás Rákosi") (r. 1947–1956), a [Stalinist](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") who was beholden to the USSR.[[18]](#cite_note-20) To ensure ideological compliance within his Stalinist government, Rákosi used the ÁVH to purge 7,000 politically dissident [Titoists](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism") and [Trotskyists](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") from the Communist Party of Hungary, for being "Western agents" whose participation in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") (1936–1939) interfered with Stalin's long-term plans for [world communism](/wiki/World_communism "World communism").[[19]](#cite_note-Litván_1996-21)[[20]](#cite_note-Tokes317-22)[[21]](#cite_note-wilson-23) Among the Stalinist governments of the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc"), the Rákosi government of the Hungarian People's Republic was most repressive of political, sexual, and religious minorities.[[13]](#cite_note-UNPara47-15)[[22]](#cite_note-lawsoc-24)[[23]](#cite_note-hungarianspectrum.org-25)[[24]](#cite_note-jta.org-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/R%C3%A1kosi_M%C3%A1ty%C3%A1s_fortepan_56324.jpg/250px-R%C3%A1kosi_M%C3%A1ty%C3%A1s_fortepan_56324.jpg)](/wiki/File:R%C3%A1kosi_M%C3%A1ty%C3%A1s_fortepan_56324.jpg)

[Mátyás Rákosi](/wiki/M%C3%A1ty%C3%A1s_R%C3%A1kosi "Mátyás Rákosi") addresses an audience in Budapest, 1948.

In 1949, the Rákosi government arrested, tortured and convicted Cardinal [József Mindszenty](/wiki/J%C3%B3zsef_Mindszenty "József Mindszenty") in a show trial[[25]](#cite_note-27) for [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason") against Hungary, for [collaboration](/wiki/Collaboration_with_Nazi_Germany_and_Fascist_Italy "Collaboration with Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy") with Nazi Germany in [the Holocaust in Hungary](/wiki/The_Holocaust_in_Hungary "The Holocaust in Hungary") – the religious persecution of Hungarian Jews, and the political persecutions of Hungarian communists and of Hungarian [anti-Nazis](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism").[[23]](#cite_note-hungarianspectrum.org-25)[[24]](#cite_note-jta.org-26) Mindszenty had, in fact, been [an active opponent of the Nazi government](/wiki/Hungary_in_World_War_II#Resistance_movement "Hungary in World War II"), encouraged [Hungarian Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Hungary "Catholic Church in Hungary") not to [vote](/wiki/1939_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "1939 Hungarian parliamentary election") for the [Arrow Cross Party](/wiki/Arrow_Cross_Party "Arrow Cross Party"), and was imprisoned for this dissent by the Arrow Cross-led Government of National Unity.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

#### Political repression

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Political repression")]

In the 1950–1952 period, the ÁVH forcibly relocated more than 26,000 non-communist Hungarians and confiscated their housing for members of the Communist Party of Hungary, and so eliminated the political threats posed by the nationalist and anti-communist [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia") and by the local [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie"). According to their particular politics, anti-communist Hungarians were either imprisoned in [concentration camps](/wiki/Concentration_camp "Concentration camp") or were [deported to the USSR](/wiki/Population_transfer_in_the_Soviet_Union "Population transfer in the Soviet Union") or were killed, either summarily or after a [show trial](/wiki/Show_trial "Show trial"); the victims included the communist politician [László Rajk](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Rajk "László Rajk"), the minister of the interior who established the ÁVH secret police.[[20]](#cite_note-Tokes317-22)[[22]](#cite_note-lawsoc-24)

The Rákosi government politicised the education system with a *toiling intelligentsia* who would assist in the Russification of Hungary; thus the study of Russian language and communist political instruction were mandatory at school and at university; religious schools were nationalized and church leaders replaced with communist officials.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)

#### Economic decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economic decline")]

In the early 1950s, the Rákosi government's socialist economics increased the per capita income of the Hungarian people, yet their standard of living diminished because of the compulsory financial contributions towards the [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") of Hungary, which reduced the [disposable and discretionary income](/wiki/Disposable_income "Disposable income") of individual Hungarian workers. That poor economy was further diminished by the bureaucratic mismanagement of resources, which caused shortages of supplies, and the consequent rationing of bread and sugar, flour and meat, et cetera.[[29]](#cite_note-31) The net result of those economic conditions, was that the Hungarian workers' disposable income in 1952 was two-thirds of the disposable income of Hungarian workers in 1938.[[30]](#cite_note-transformation-32)

Besides paying for the Red Army occupation of socialist Hungary, the Hungarians also paid [war reparations](/wiki/War_reparations "War reparations") (US$300 million) to the USSR, to the [Czechoslovak Socialist Republic](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Socialist_Republic "Czechoslovak Socialist Republic"), and to the [Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia").[[31]](#cite_note-33) In 1946, the [Hungarian National Bank](/wiki/Hungarian_National_Bank "Hungarian National Bank") reported that the cost of war reparations was "between 19 and 22 per cent of the annual national income" of Hungary,[[32]](#cite_note-34) an onerous national expense aggravated by the [hyperinflation](/wiki/Hyperinflation "Hyperinflation") consequent to the post-war [depreciation](/wiki/Depreciation "Depreciation") of the [Hungarian pengő](/wiki/Hungarian_peng%C5%91 "Hungarian pengő").[[33]](#cite_note-35) Moreover, participation in the Soviet-sponsored Council of Mutual Economic Assistance ([COMECON](/wiki/Comecon "Comecon")) prevented direct trade with the countries of the West, and also prevented the Hungarian People's Republic from receiving American financial aid through the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") (1948).[[34]](#cite_note-36) Socially, the imposition of Soviet-style economic policies and the payment of war reparations angered the peoples of Hungary, while the cumulative effects of [economic austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") fuelled anti-Soviet political discontent as the payment of foreign debt took precedence over the material needs of the Hungarian people.[[35]](#cite_note-LibCongressHungaryEconomy-37)

### International events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Nagy_Imre_fortepan_74215.jpg/250px-Nagy_Imre_fortepan_74215.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagy_Imre_fortepan_74215.jpg)

In the Eastern bloc, Hungarian Prime Minister [Imre Nagy](/wiki/Imre_Nagy "Imre Nagy") (centre) proved too progressive a Communist in his government's pursuit of the Hungarian road to Socialism, taking it too far out of the Soviet Union's orbit (October 1956).

#### De–Stalinization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: De–Stalinization")]

On 5 March 1953, the death of [Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") allowed the Communist Party of the Soviet Union (CPSU) to proceed with the [de-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization") of the USSR, which was a relative liberalization of politics that afterward allowed most European communist parties and the communist parties of the Warsaw Pact to develop a [reformist wing](/wiki/Reformism "Reformism") – within the structures of the [philosophy of Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") and orders from Moscow. Hence, the reformist communist [Imre Nagy](/wiki/Imre_Nagy "Imre Nagy") became prime minister (1953–55) of the Hungarian People's Republic, in replacement of the Stalinist Mátyás Rákosi (1952–53), whose heavy-hand style of communist government had proved counter-productive to the interests of the USSR in Hungary.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

Despite not being prime minister of Hungary, Rákosi remained politically powerful as the General Secretary of the Hungarian Communist Party, and so undermined many of the Nagy government's political and socio-economic reforms; by 18 April 1955, Rákosi had so discredited Nagy that the USSR deposed Nagy as head-of-state of Socialist Hungary. Three months of plotting and conspiring rid Rákosi and the Hungarian Communist Party of Imre Nagy, who was reduced to a political non-person. On 14 April Prime Minister Imre Nagy was stripped of his Hungarian Communist Party offices and functions and was sacked as prime minister on 18 April. Despite having been out of political favor with the USSR since January 1955, Nagy had refused to perform the requisite communist penance of '[self-criticism](/wiki/Criticism_and_self-criticism_(Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism) "Criticism and self-criticism (Marxism–Leninism)")', and refused to resign as PM of Hungary.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

In February 1956, as the First Secretary of the CPSU, Nikita Khrushchev initiated the de-Stalinization of the USSR with the speech *[On the Personality Cult and its Consequences](/wiki/On_the_Cult_of_Personality_and_Its_Consequences "On the Cult of Personality and Its Consequences")*, which catalogued and denounced the abuses of power committed by Stalin and his inner-circle protégés, in Russia and abroad.[[38]](#cite_note-40) Therefore, the de-Stalinization of Hungarian People's Republic featured Rákosi's dismissal as General Secretary of the Communist Party of Hungary, and his replacement by [Ernő Gerő](/wiki/Ern%C5%91_Ger%C5%91 "Ernő Gerő"), on 18 July 1956.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

From the Western perspective, the countries of western Europe co-operated with the CIA's propaganda radio network, [Radio Free Europe](/wiki/Radio_Free_Europe/Radio_Liberty "Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty"), to broadcast the speech *On the Personality Cult and its Consequences* to the countries of eastern Europe, expecting that, as Secretary of the CPSU, Khrushchev's anti–Stalinist speech would substantively contribute to the [destabilisation](/wiki/Destabilisation "Destabilisation") of the internal politics of the Warsaw Pact countries.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

#### The establishing of the Warsaw Pact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The establishing of the Warsaw Pact")]

On 14 May 1955, with the [Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact"), the USSR established the Warsaw Pact with seven countries of the Eastern Bloc, including the Hungarian People's Republic. The geopolitical principles of the Warsaw Pact defence treaty included "respect for the independence and [the] sovereignty of [the member] states" and the practise of "non-interference in their internal affairs".[[41]](#cite_note-43) Then, on 15 May 1955, a day after the USSR established the Warsaw Pact, the [Austrian State Treaty](/wiki/Austrian_State_Treaty "Austrian State Treaty") established Austria as a neutral country in the geopolitical cold war between the US and the USSR.[[42]](#cite_note-44) Austria's declaration of geopolitical neutrality allowed the Communist government of PM Nagy to publicly consider "the possibility of Hungary adopting a neutral status on the Austrian pattern".[[43]](#cite_note-45)

In June 1956, the [Polish Army](/wiki/Polish_Land_Forces "Polish Land Forces") violently repressed the [workers' uprising at Poznań](/wiki/1956_Pozna%C5%84_protests "1956 Poznań protests") against the economic policies of the [Polish People's Republic](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic"). In October, the Polish government appointed the [politically rehabilitated](/wiki/Political_rehabilitation "Political rehabilitation"), reform communist [Władysław Gomułka](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Gomu%C5%82ka "Władysław Gomułka") as First Secretary of the [Polish United Workers' Party](/wiki/Polish_United_Workers%27_Party "Polish United Workers' Party") to deal with the USSR, and, by 19 October 1956, Gomułka had successfully negotiated greater trade agreements and fewer Red Army troops stationed in Poland.[[44]](#cite_note-satellite-46) The USSR's concessions to Poland – known as the [Polish October](/wiki/Polish_October "Polish October") – emboldened Hungarians to demand from the USSR like concessions for the Hungarian People's Republic, which much contributed to the Hungarians' greatly idealistic politics in October 1956.[[45]](#cite_note-Machcewicz-47)

On 13 October 1956, a group of 12 students from the university faculties in [Szeged](/wiki/Szeged "Szeged") met to play cards, and snubbed the DISZ, the official communist student union, by re-establishing the MEFESZ (Union of Hungarian University and Academy Students), the democratic student union banned by the Stalinist Rákosi government.[[17]](#cite_note-Crampton295-19) The MEFESZ students distributed hand-written notes in classrooms to inform faculty and students of the time and place for the meeting on 16 October 1956. A professor of law was chairman of committee who formally reestablished the MEFESZ student union, with the proclamation and publication of a twenty-demand manifesto, ten demands about the MEFESZ, and ten anti-Soviet demands, e.g. free elections and the departure of Soviet troops from Hungary, etc.; days later, the university students at [Pécs](/wiki/P%C3%A9cs "Pécs"), [Miskolc](/wiki/Miskolc "Miskolc"), and [Sopron](/wiki/Sopron "Sopron") followed suit.

On 22 October, at the Budapest University of Technology and Economics, one of the law students from the original group of twelve students, announced that the MEFESZ student union was again politically active, and then proclaimed the [Sixteen Political, Economic, and Ideological Points](/wiki/Demands_of_Hungarian_Revolutionaries_of_1956 "Demands of Hungarian Revolutionaries of 1956") against the USSR's geopolitical hegemony upon Hungary.[[46]](#cite_note-sixteen-48) On 23 October the [Hungarian Writers' Union](/wiki/Hungarian_Writers%27_Union "Hungarian Writers' Union") ceremoniously proclaimed Hungary's anti-Soviet political solidarity with anti-communist reformers in Poland when they laid a commemorative wreath at the statue of the Polish hero [Gen. Józef Zachariasz Bem](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Bem "Józef Bem") who also was a hero of the [Hungarian Revolution of 1848](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1848 "Hungarian Revolution of 1848"); likewise, the MEFESZ student union held a parallel demonstration of Hungarians' political solidarity with the Poles.[[45]](#cite_note-Machcewicz-47)[[47]](#cite_note-UN-49)

### Initiating gunfire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Initiating gunfire")]

The peaceful demonstration of students started at about 2 pm at the Petőfi square. An actor, Imre Sinkovits recited the National song (a poem by Sándor Petőfi), then a university student read out the 16 demands of the protesters.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

In the afternoon of 23 October 1956, approximately 20,000 protestors met beside the statue of General [Józef Bem](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Bem "Józef Bem"), a national hero of Poland and Hungary.[[49]](#cite_note-51) To the amassed crowd of protestors, the intellectual [Péter Veres](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_Veres_(politician) "Péter Veres (politician)"), the president of the Writers' Union (*Írószövetség*), read a manifesto demanding Hungarian independence from all foreign powers; a [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") political system based upon [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform") and (public) [state ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership") in the [economy](/wiki/Economy "Economy"); Hungarian membership to the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"); and all [freedoms and rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") for the citizens of Hungary.[[50]](#cite_note-52) After Veres proclaimed the manifesto demanding Hungarian sovereignty, the crowd chanted the Hungarian patriotic poem [National Song (*Nemzeti dal*)](/wiki/Nemzeti_dal "Nemzeti dal"), which the Soviet-controlled Rákosi government of Hungary had banned from public performance; the crowd repeatedly chanted the refrain: "This we swear, this we swear, that we will no longer be slaves."[[51]](#cite_note-Heller-53)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Szt%C3%A1lin_szobor_Budapest_1956.jpg/250px-Szt%C3%A1lin_szobor_Budapest_1956.jpg)](/wiki/File:Szt%C3%A1lin_szobor_Budapest_1956.jpg)

In Budapest, anti-communists and nationalists place a Hungarian national flag atop a demolished statue of Josef Stalin.

At 20:00, the first secretary of the Hungarian Working People's Party, Ernő Gerő broadcast a hardline speech condemning the political demands of the intelligentsia and of the university students.[[52]](#cite_note-UN_report_2C_p55-54) Angered by Gerő's rejection, some protestors realised one of their demands, and demolished the [Stalin Monument in Budapest](/wiki/Stalin_Monument_(Budapest) "Stalin Monument (Budapest)"), which had been erected in place of a razed church, in 1951;[[53]](#cite_note-55) and, by 21:30 hrs – an hour and a half later – the nationalist and anti-communist protestors had destroyed the eight-metre-tall statue of Josef Stalin.[[52]](#cite_note-UN_report_2C_p55-54)

Also at 20:00, a crowd of nationalist and anti-communist protestors had gathered outside the [Magyar Rádió](/wiki/Magyar_R%C3%A1di%C3%B3 "Magyar Rádió") building, which was guarded by the ÁVH secret police. Violence soon occurred between the sides when the protestors heard rumours of the arrest and detainment of a delegation of students who had entered the radio station in effort to broadcast their political demands to the entire country. The situation escalated after protestors heard rumours that the ÁVH had killed their delegation of comrades sent to broadcast the anti–Soviet message. In response, from the windows of the building, the ÁVH threw tear gas grenades at and fired upon the many anti-communist and nationalist protestors assembled outside the Magyar Rádió building, and soon required resupply and reinforcement.[[54]](#cite_note-UN_report_2C_p56-56)

In suppressing the Hungarians' anti-government protestations, the ÁVH smuggled weapons and ammunition in an ambulance for delivery to them at the Magyar Rádió building, but the protestors hijacked the ambulance and the weapons for themselves. The Hungarian Army sent soldiers to support the ÁVH policemen defending the Magyar Rádió building, but some of the soldiers tore off the red-star insignia on their caps and joined the side of the anti-government protestors.[[51]](#cite_note-Heller-53)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[54]](#cite_note-UN_report_2C_p56-56) ÁVH policemen used guns and tear gas, while the protestors set police cars afire and distributed weapons captured from the military and police forces, and acted on their anti–Soviet politics by destroying the symbols of Russian communism in Hungary.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

### Deposing the communist government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Deposing the communist government")]

On 23 October, Gerő asked for the USSR's military intervention in order "to suppress a demonstration that was reaching an ever-greater and unprecedented scale", which threatens the national security of the Hungarian People's Republic.[[44]](#cite_note-satellite-46) To that end, the USSR already had planned the invasion and occupation of Hungary, and the political purging of Hungarian society.[[56]](#cite_note-58) At 02:00 on 24 October 1956, Soviet defence minister [Georgy Zhukov](/wiki/Georgy_Zhukov "Georgy Zhukov") ordered the Red Army to occupy Budapest – the capital city of a Warsaw Pact country.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Kossuth_Lajos_utca_a_Ferenciek_tere_fel%C5%91l_n%C3%A9zve._1956._okt%C3%B3ber_25-e_d%C3%A9lut%C3%A1n%2C_-_Fortepan_24652.jpg/250px-Kossuth_Lajos_utca_a_Ferenciek_tere_fel%C5%91l_n%C3%A9zve._1956._okt%C3%B3ber_25-e_d%C3%A9lut%C3%A1n%2C_-_Fortepan_24652.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kossuth_Lajos_utca_a_Ferenciek_tere_fel%C5%91l_n%C3%A9zve._1956._okt%C3%B3ber_25-e_d%C3%A9lut%C3%A1n,_-_Fortepan_24652.jpg)

Kossuth Lajos Street seen from Ferenciek Square: anti-Soviet demonstrators march in protest against the USSR's control of Hungary, 25 October 1956.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_p%C3%A1pa_%28K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g%29_t%C3%A9r%2C_a_p%C3%A1rth%C3%A1z_ostromakor_kiv%C3%A9gzett_v%C3%A9d%C5%91_holtteste._Fortepan_24462.jpg/250px-II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_p%C3%A1pa_%28K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g%29_t%C3%A9r%2C_a_p%C3%A1rth%C3%A1z_ostromakor_kiv%C3%A9gzett_v%C3%A9d%C5%91_holtteste._Fortepan_24462.jpg)](/wiki/File:II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_p%C3%A1pa_(K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g)_t%C3%A9r,_a_p%C3%A1rth%C3%A1z_ostromakor_kiv%C3%A9gzett_v%C3%A9d%C5%91_holtteste._Fortepan_24462.jpg)

The corpse of a communist officer killed defending the headquarters of the Hungarian Communist Party, Republic Square, Budapest.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Jack_Metzger_%E2%80%93_Ungarischer_Freiheitskampf%2C_1956_%28Com_M05-0448-0001%29.jpg/250px-Jack_Metzger_%E2%80%93_Ungarischer_Freiheitskampf%2C_1956_%28Com_M05-0448-0001%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jack_Metzger_%E2%80%93_Ungarischer_Freiheitskampf,_1956_(Com_M05-0448-0001).jpg)

Anti-communist Hungarian revolutionaries amid the damaged buildings of Budapest, November 1956.

By 12:00 on 24 October, Red Army tanks were stationed outside the parliament building, and Red Army soldiers held the bridges and crossroads that controlled access to Budapest, while Hungarian revolutionaries barricaded streets to defend their city from the Red Army.[[51]](#cite_note-Heller-53) Also on that day, Imre Nagy became prime minister in place of [András Hegedüs](/wiki/Andr%C3%A1s_Heged%C3%BCs "András Hegedüs").[[58]](#cite_note-60) In a national radio broadcast, Nagy asked a [ceasefire](/wiki/Ceasefire "Ceasefire") between the Red Army and the Hungarian Revolutionaries, and agreed to initiate postponed political reforms decided in 1953. Despite the pleas of Nagy, groups of revolutionaries in Budapest armed themselves and continually fought the Red Army.[[59]](#cite_note-Chapter_II.C,_para_57_p._20-61)

At around 3 p.m. on October 24, there were still approximately four thousand armed demonstrators at the Radio building. They demanded the removal of Ernő Gerő. A. Mikoyan and M. Suslov communicated this fact to the party leadership in Moscow in a telegram.[[60]](#cite_note-62)

At the offices of the communist newspaper *[Szabad Nép](/wiki/Szabad_N%C3%A9p "Szabad Nép")*, the ÁVH guards fired upon unarmed protestors; in turn, anti-communists attacked and drove out the ÁVH policemen from the newspaper building.[[59]](#cite_note-Chapter_II.C,_para_57_p._20-61) The Hungarian revolutionaries then avenged themselves against the ÁVH policemen.[[61]](#cite_note-63) On 25 October, a crowd gathered in Kossuth Square in front of the [Hungarian Parliament Building](/wiki/Hungarian_Parliament_Building "Hungarian Parliament Building"), which was a mixed group of civilian demonstrators including women, children, and elderly. Most of them came from the mass demonstration outside the [Astoria](/wiki/Astoria,_Budapest "Astoria, Budapest"), accompanied by three Soviet tanks, which the demonstrators climbed on, fraternising with the crews. At the same time a column of Soviet tanks carrying two delegates of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union (CPSU) sent to Hungary, Politburo members [Anastas Mikoyan](/wiki/Anastas_Mikoyan "Anastas Mikoyan") and [Mikhail Suslov](/wiki/Mikhail_Suslov "Mikhail Suslov"), as well as KGB Chairman [Ivan Serov](/wiki/Ivan_Serov "Ivan Serov") and [Mikhail Malinin](/wiki/Mikhail_Malinin "Mikhail Malinin"), commander-in-chief of the Soviet forces in Hungary were on their way to the headquarters of the Hungarian Workers' Party (MDP) on Akadémia Street, where they were to attend a meeting of the Central Executive Committee (CEC), which was to begin at 10:00. Under pressure from the Soviet delegates, the CEC relieved Ernő Gerő of his position as party leader and elected [János Kádár](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_K%C3%A1d%C3%A1r "János Kádár") in his place. Meanwhile, the crowd demonstrating at Kossuth Square elected a delegation to deliver their demands in the Parliament, but government guards and a company of the Ministry of Defence's armoured regiment, backed by seven Soviet T-54 tanks lined up in front of the Parliament, kept the demonstrators away from the building. The events at Astoria were repeated, with demonstrators trying to make friends with the Soviet soldiers, handing out bilingual leaflets and some climbing on top of the guard tanks. At a few minutes after 11:00, General Serov decided to inspect the situation on Kossuth Square, accompanied by several Soviet and Hungarian officers and a tank. Seeing several Soviet soldiers fraternising with the demonstrators, Serov ordered the firing of warning shots, which caused a mass panic on the square. In the ensuing confusion, the Soviet tanks lined up in front of the Parliament building responded by targeting the buildings on the other side of the square. Another Soviet tank coming from a nearby street fired aimed shots at the demonstrators trying to take cover. This action claimed the most lives. The number of the dead is still disputed, with estimates ranging from 75 to 1000.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

According to another version of the events, which already appeared during the revolution but still unproven, the ÁVH policemen and/or communist partisans fired into the assembled protestors from the roof of the building of the Agricultural Ministry.[[63]](#cite_note-Chapter_X.I,_para_482_p._153-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66) In the fog of war, some Red Army soldiers mistakenly returned the fire towards the roof, having mistakenly believed themselves the targets.[[51]](#cite_note-Heller-53)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[65]](#cite_note-67) The Hungarian revolutionaries armed themselves with weapons captured from ÁVH policemen and with weapons donated by anti-communist soldiers who had [deserted](/wiki/Desertion "Desertion") the [Hungarian People's Army](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Army "Hungarian People's Army") for the Hungarian Revolution against the USSR; from amongst the crowd outside the parliament, the armed revolutionaries shot at the roof-top ÁVH policemen.[[51]](#cite_note-Heller-53)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[63]](#cite_note-Chapter_X.I,_para_482_p._153-65)

Meanwhile, nationalist and anti-communist loyalties had fractured the chain of command of the Hungarian Army in response to the communist government's order to militarily repress the popular demonstrations against the Soviet control of Hungary. The Hungarian Army units in Budapest and in the countryside remained uninvolved in the Revolution, because local commanders avoided politics in order to avoid repressing the revolutionaries.[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Order was restored in the 24–29 October period after the Hungarian Army had fought 71 firefights with the nationalist and with the anti-communist revolutionaries in 50 communities.[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] On 26 October, in the town of Kecskemét, outside the office of State Security and the local jail, Hungarian Army's Third Corps, led by Major General [Lajos Gyurkó](/w/index.php?title=Lajos_Gyurk%C3%B3&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lajos Gyurkó (page does not exist)") [[hu](https://hu.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gyurk%C3%B3_Lajos_(katonatiszt) "hu:Gyurkó Lajos (katonatiszt)")], shot seven anti-communist protestors and arrested the organizers of the anti-Soviet protest.[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] On Gyurkó's order Hungarian Air Force fighter planes shot up demonstrators with cannon fire in various towns (see [hu:Tiszakécskei sortűz](https://hu.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tiszak%C3%A9cskei_sort%C5%B1z "hu:Tiszakécskei sortűz")), earning the praise of János Kádár after the defeat of the "counter-revolution" as "the only division commander who, at the call of the party organisation, swept the Danube-Tisza Interfluve six times, smashing everything".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Protesters_with_body_of_AVH_personnel.jpg/250px-Protesters_with_body_of_AVH_personnel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protesters_with_body_of_AVH_personnel.jpg)

Protesters kicking the body of a slain ÁVH agent

As Hungarian revolutionaries fought the soldiers and tanks of the Red Army with small arms and [Molotov cocktails](/wiki/Molotov_cocktail "Molotov cocktail") in the streets of Budapest, throughout Hungary, revolutionary [workers' councils](/wiki/Workers%27_council "Workers' council") assumed government power and called [general strikes](/wiki/General_strike "General strike") to halt the economy and the functioning of civil society. In ridding Hungary of the influence of and control from the USSR, the revolutionaries destroyed the symbols of Communism, such as the [red star](/wiki/Red_star "Red star") and Red Army monuments, and burned communist literature. Moreover, Revolutionary militias, such as the 400-man militia led by [József Dudás](/wiki/J%C3%B3zsef_Dud%C3%A1s "József Dudás") attacked and murdered pro-Soviet Hungarians and ÁVH policemen.[[67]](#cite_note-69) The Hungarian Army armoured division stationed in Budapest, commanded by [Pál Maléter](/wiki/P%C3%A1l_Mal%C3%A9ter "Pál Maléter") led the Hungarian Revolution against the USSR's control of Hungary, and negotiated ceasefire agreements with the revolutionaries;[[68]](#cite_note-70) nonetheless, the Hungarian Revolution took many communist prisoners who were registered to lists that identified the prisoner either for summary execution or as an [enemy of the people](/wiki/Enemy_of_the_people "Enemy of the people").[[69]](#cite_note-71)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_%28K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g%29_t%C3%A9r%2C_a_p%C3%A1rth%C3%A1zhoz_k%C3%B6t%C5%91d%C5%91_felt%C3%A9telezett_kazamat%C3%A1k_keres%C3%A9se._Fortepan_24518.jpg/250px-II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_%28K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g%29_t%C3%A9r%2C_a_p%C3%A1rth%C3%A1zhoz_k%C3%B6t%C5%91d%C5%91_felt%C3%A9telezett_kazamat%C3%A1k_keres%C3%A9se._Fortepan_24518.jpg)](/wiki/File:II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_(K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g)_t%C3%A9r,_a_p%C3%A1rth%C3%A1zhoz_k%C3%B6t%C5%91d%C5%91_felt%C3%A9telezett_kazamat%C3%A1k_keres%C3%A9se._Fortepan_24518.jpg)

Suspected ÁVH agents digging at [Republic Square](/wiki/Liberty_Square_(Budapest) "Liberty Square (Budapest)") in search of mass grave of Stalinist repressions and underground escape tunnels

In the [Csepel](/wiki/Csepel "Csepel") area of Budapest, 250 communists defended the Csepel Iron and Steel Works, and, on 27 October, the Hungarian Army restored order in Csepel; two days later, the Hungarian Revolutionaries recaptured Csepel after the Hungarian Army's withdrawal on 29 October. In the [Angyalföld](/wiki/Angyalf%C3%B6ld "Angyalföld") area of Budapest, the communists and 350 armed workers and 380 communist soldiers defended the Láng factory. Anti-fascist Hungarian veterans of the Second World War participated in recapturing the offices of the *Szabad Nép* communist newspaper. At the town of [Szarvas](/wiki/Szarvas "Szarvas"), armed guards defended the Hungarian Communist Party and the communist government of Hungary.[[70]](#cite_note-72)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In the event, the Revolutionaries' successful attacks upon the Parliament collapsed the communist government of Hungary;[[71]](#cite_note-73) and Gerő and ex-PM András Hegedüs fled Hungary to the USSR; Nagy became prime minister, and János Kádár became the first secretary of the Hungarian Communist Party.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

The Nagy government freed the political prisoner General [Béla Király](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Kir%C3%A1ly "Béla Király") to restore order to Hungary with a National Guard force composed of policemen, soldiers, and Revolutionaries loyal to Hungary.[[73]](#cite_note-ng-75) On 30 October 1956, Király's National Guard attacked the building of the Central Committee of the Hungarian Communist Party and killed every pro-Soviet officer of the Hungarian Communist Party, ÁVH policeman, and pro-Soviet Hungarian soldier they encountered;[[74]](#cite_note-76) and most Red Army troops withdrew from Budapest to garrisons in the Hungarian countryside.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

### Interlude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Interlude")]

Fighting ceased between 28 October and 4 November, as many Hungarians believed that Soviet military units were withdrawing from Hungary.[[76]](#cite_note-78) According to post-revolution communist sources, there were approximately 213 Hungarian Working People's Party members executed during the period.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

#### New government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: New government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Flyer._Imre_Nagy%2C_Head_of_government_-1956.10.27.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Flyer._Imre_Nagy%2C_Head_of_government_-1956.10.27.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flyer._Imre_Nagy,_Head_of_government_-1956.10.27.tiff)

The political flyer announces: A New Government Has Been Formed. Prime Minister Imre Nagy is head of the government, 27 October 1956.

The new Nagy government was surprised by the rapidity with which the Hungarian Revolution extended from the streets of Budapest to all of Hungary, and the consequent collapse of the old Gerő–Hegedüs communist government. As head of government, Nagy asked every Hungarian to exercise political forbearance in order to restore civil order to Hungary. As the only Communist Hungarian leader with political credibility among Hungarians, the political actions of the Nagy government allowed the USSR to view the Hungarians' anti-Soviet protests as a popular uprising, rather than as an anti-communist [counter-revolution](/wiki/Counter-revolutionary "Counter-revolutionary").[[78]](#cite_note-80) On 28 October 1956, the Nagy government announced the ceasefire among the nationalist, the anti-communist, and the Communist Hungarians, and that, to resolve the national crisis, Nagy would:

* consider the revolt as a "great, national and democratic event", and not an anti-communist counter-revolution
* effect an unconditional ceasefire and grant political amnesty for the revolutionaries
* negotiate with the revolutionaries
* disband the ÁVH security police
* establish a national guard for Hungary
* arrange the immediate withdrawal of the Red Army from Budapest and from Hungary [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 1 November, the Nagy government formally declared Hungary's withdrawal from the Warsaw Pact and Hungary's international status as a politically non-aligned country.[[79]](#cite_note-81)[[80]](#cite_note-82)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Because of being in power for only ten days, the National Government did not explain their policies in detail; however, contemporary newspaper editorials stressed that Hungary should be a multiparty [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") uninvolved in the Russo–American Cold War.[[81]](#cite_note-83) About 8,000 political prisoners were released, most notably Cardinal [József Mindszenty](/wiki/J%C3%B3zsef_Mindszenty "József Mindszenty").[[82]](#cite_note-IC-84)[[83]](#cite_note-85) Banned political parties, such as the Independent Smallholders and the [National Peasant Party](/wiki/National_Peasant_Party_(Hungary) "National Peasant Party (Hungary)") ("Petőfi Party"),[[84]](#cite_note-VM-86) reappeared to join the coalition.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Kossuth_Lajos_utca_-_K%C3%A1roly_%28Tan%C3%A1cs%29_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_sarok._Fortepan_23591.jpg/250px-Kossuth_Lajos_utca_-_K%C3%A1roly_%28Tan%C3%A1cs%29_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_sarok._Fortepan_23591.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kossuth_Lajos_utca_-_K%C3%A1roly_(Tan%C3%A1cs)_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_sarok._Fortepan_23591.jpg)

A crowd cheers Hungarian troops in Budapest.

In 1,170 communities in Hungary, there were 348 cases of revolutionary councils dismissing the local administrators; 312 cases of revolutionary councils sacking the bosses; and 215 cases of the locals burning the communist administrative records of their communities. In 681 communities, anti-communist and nationalist Hungarians damaged and destroyed symbols of the hegemony of the USSR, such as the Red Star, and statues of Stalin and of Lenin; 393 communities damaged Soviet war memorials; and 122 communities burned the books of Marx, Lenin, and Stalin.[[19]](#cite_note-Litván_1996-21)[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Local revolutionary councils formed throughout Hungary,[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) generally without involvement from the preoccupied National Government in Budapest, and assumed various responsibilities of local government from the defunct Communist party.[[90]](#cite_note-rc-92) By 30 October, the councils had been officially sanctioned by the Hungarian Working People's Party, and the Nagy government asked for their support as "autonomous, democratic local organs formed during the Revolution".[[90]](#cite_note-rc-92) Likewise, [workers' councils](/wiki/Workers%27_council "Workers' council") were established at industrial plants and mines, and many unpopular regulations such as production norms were eliminated. The workers' councils strove to manage the enterprise while protecting workers' interests, establishing a socialist economy free of rigid party control.[[91]](#cite_note-93) Local control by the councils was not always bloodless; in [Debrecen](/wiki/Debrecen "Debrecen"), [Győr](/wiki/Gy%C5%91r "Győr"), Sopron, [Mosonmagyaróvár](/wiki/Mosonmagyar%C3%B3v%C3%A1r "Mosonmagyaróvár") and other cities, crowds of demonstrators were fired upon by the ÁVH, with many lives lost. The ÁVH were disarmed, often by force, in many cases assisted by the local police.[[90]](#cite_note-rc-92)

In total, there were approximately 2,100 local revolutionary and workers councils with over 28,000 members. The councils held a combined conference in Budapest that decided to end the nationwide labour strikes and to resume work on 5 November, with the more important councils sending delegates to the Parliament to assure the Nagy government of their support.[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

#### Soviet perspective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Soviet perspective")]

On 24 October 1956, the Politburo of the USSR discussed how to resolve the political revolts that had occurred in Warsaw Pact countries, specifically the Polish October and the Hungarian Revolt. Led by [Vyacheslav Molotov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Molotov "Vyacheslav Molotov"), the hardline faction of the CPSU voted for military intervention, but were opposed by Khrushchev and Marshal Georgy Zhukov who sought a political resolution to the Hungarian revolt. In Budapest, the Soviet delegation reported to Moscow that the Hungarian political situation was less confrontational than reported during the revolt proper. In pursuit of political resolution, Khrushchev said that Gerő's 23 October request for Soviet intervention indicated that the Hungarian Communist Party retained the confidence of the Hungarian people, because the Hungarians were protesting unresolved socio-economic problems, not ideology.[[44]](#cite_note-satellite-46) Meanwhile, in the West, the concurrent [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") (29 October – 7 November 1956) of the French and the British empires' seizure of the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") from Egypt voided the political possibility of Western military intervention to Hungary. On 28 October, Khrushchev said that Soviet military intervention to Hungary would be a mistaken imitation of the Anglo–French intervention to Egypt.[[92]](#cite_note-mastny200203-94)

In the Soviet Union, on 30 October 1956, the Presidium of the CPSU decided to not depose the new Hungarian government.[[93]](#cite_note-95)[[94]](#cite_note-96) Zhukov said: "We should withdraw troops from Budapest, and, if necessary, withdraw from Hungary, as a whole. This is a lesson for us in the military-political sphere". The Presidium then adopted and published the *Declaration of the Government of the USSR on the Principles of Development and Further Strengthening of Friendship and Cooperation between the Soviet Union and other Socialist States*, which said that "The Soviet Government is prepared to enter into the appropriate negotiations with the government of the Hungarian People's Republic, and [with] other members of the Warsaw Treaty, on the question of the presence of Soviet troops in the territory of Hungary."[[95]](#cite_note-97)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Szent_Istv%C3%A1n_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_a_Falk_Miksa_%28N%C3%A9phadsereg%29_utca_fel%C5%91l_a_Honv%C3%A9d_utca_fel%C3%A9_n%C3%A9zve._A_szovjet_csapatok_ideiglenes_kivonul%C3%A1sa_1956._okt%C3%B3ber_31-%C3%A9n._Fortepan_24787.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Szent_Istv%C3%A1n_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_a_Falk_Miksa_(N%C3%A9phadsereg)_utca_fel%C5%91l_a_Honv%C3%A9d_utca_fel%C3%A9_n%C3%A9zve._A_szovjet_csapatok_ideiglenes_kivonul%C3%A1sa_1956._okt%C3%B3ber_31-%C3%A9n._Fortepan_24787.jpg)

The Soviet repression of the Hungarian Revolution saw [T-54](/wiki/T-54/T-55 "T-54/T-55") tanks patrolling the streets of Budapest, until the Red Army temporarily withdrew on 31 October 1956.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_p%C3%A1pa_%28K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g%29_t%C3%A9r%2C_MDP_Budapesti_P%C3%A1rtbizotts%C3%A1g%C3%A1nak_sz%C3%A9kh%C3%A1za._Fortepan_24495.jpg/250px-II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_p%C3%A1pa_%28K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g%29_t%C3%A9r%2C_MDP_Budapesti_P%C3%A1rtbizotts%C3%A1g%C3%A1nak_sz%C3%A9kh%C3%A1za._Fortepan_24495.jpg)](/wiki/File:II._J%C3%A1nos_P%C3%A1l_p%C3%A1pa_(K%C3%B6zt%C3%A1rsas%C3%A1g)_t%C3%A9r,_MDP_Budapesti_P%C3%A1rtbizotts%C3%A1g%C3%A1nak_sz%C3%A9kh%C3%A1za._Fortepan_24495.jpg)

The damaged headquarters of the Hungarian Communist Party, on Köztársaság tér, in Budapest.

In Hungary, on 30 October, consequent to hearing rumours of that the secret police had anti-communist prisoners, and rumours of the ÁVH shooting anti-communist demonstrators in the city of Mosonmagyaróvár, armed protestors attacked the ÁVH detachment guarding the headquarters building of the Hungarian Working People's Party in Köztársaság tér (Republic Square), in Budapest.[[90]](#cite_note-rc-92)[[96]](#cite_note-Kramer1-98)[[97]](#cite_note-revhu-99) The anti-communists killed more than 20 ÁVH officers and ÁVH conscripts; the head of the Budapest party committee, Imre Mező, also was killed.[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-parsons-101) Within hours, news reportage and filmed scenes of the Hungarian anti-communist revolt that occurred in Republic Square were broadcast in the USSR; and the CPSU made propaganda of the images of the communist victims of the Hungarian Revolt.[[99]](#cite_note-parsons-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102) The leaders of the Hungarian Revolution condemned the attack upon the ÁVH headquarters and asked the protestors to cease and desist from mob violence.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

On 30 October, at Budapest, Anastas Mikoyan and Mikhail Suslov spoke with Nagy who told them that Hungarian geopolitical neutrality was a long-term political objective for the Hungarian People's Republic, which he wanted to discuss with the presidium of the CPSU.[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-105) Khrushchev considered the geopolitical options for the USSR's resolving the Hungarian anti-communist revolution, but Nagy's declaration of Hungarian neutrality decided his dispatching the Red Army into Hungary.[[104]](#cite_note-106) The USSR invaded the Hungarian People's Republic, because:[[105]](#cite_note-107)[[106]](#cite_note-presidium1nov-108)

* Simultaneous political movements towards multi-party, parliamentary democracy, and a democratic national council of workers might "lead towards a capitalist state" in Hungary and Poland, each movement challenged the authority of communist parties in eastern Europe.[[107]](#cite_note-109)[[108]](#cite_note-110)
* The militarists in the CPSU would not understand the USSR's failure to intervene in Hungary. That de-Stalinisation had alienated the hardline members of the CPSU, for whom anti-communist protests threatened Soviet hegemony in eastern Europe. That the [workers' uprising in East Germany](/wiki/East_German_uprising_of_1953 "East German uprising of 1953") (17 June 1953) – the repression of which killed 84 protestors and produced 700 anti-communist prisoners – required a new Communist government for the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany").[[109]](#cite_note-111) That the [workers' protests at Poznań](/wiki/1956_Pozna%C5%84_protests "1956 Poznań protests") (June 1956) – the repression of which killed 57–78 anti-communist protestors – created the Polish October movement, which installed a Polish Communist government less dependent on orders from Moscow.[[110]](#cite_note-Paczkowski-112)[[111]](#cite_note-Jastrząb-113)[[112]](#cite_note-Wójtowocz-114)
* Hungarian geopolitical neutrality and withdrawal from the Warsaw Pact breached the [buffer zone](/wiki/Buffer_zone "Buffer zone") of [satellite states](/wiki/Satellite_state "Satellite state") by which the USSR protected themselves from invasion.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

In the People's Republic of Hungary, the anti-communist militants concluded that "the [Hungarian Communist] Party is the incarnation of bureaucratic despotism" and that "socialism can develop only on the foundations of direct democracy." For the anti-communists, the struggle of the Hungarian workers was "for the principle of direct democracy" and that "all power should be transferred to the Workers Committees of Hungary."[[114]](#cite_note-James2013-116) In response, the Presidium broke the *de facto* ceasefire and repressed the Hungarian Revolution.[[115]](#cite_note-117) The Soviet Union's plan was to declare a "Provisional Revolutionary Government" led by János Kádár, who would appeal for Soviet assistance to restore order to Hungary. Kádár was in Moscow in early November, and was in communication with the Soviet embassy whilst still a member of the Nagy government.[[116]](#cite_note-118)[[117]](#cite_note-119) The USSR sent diplomatic delegations to other communist governments in Eastern Europe and to the People's Republic of China in effort to avoid misunderstandings that might provoke to regional conflicts, and broadcast propaganda explaining their second Soviet intervention to Hungary. The Soviet diplomats disguised their intentions by engaging the Nagy government in talks about withdrawing the Red Army from Hungary.[[118]](#cite_note-presidium-120)

Moreover, [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") influenced Khrushchev's decision to repress the Hungarian uprising. The deputy chairman of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"), [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi"), pressed Khrushchev to militarily repress the Hungarian Revolution.[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122) Although Sino–Soviet relations were unstable, the opinion of Mao carried great weight among the members of the Presidium of the CPSU. Initially, Mao opposed a second intervention, which was communicated to Khrushchev on 30 October, before the Presidium met and decided against a Hungarian intervention;[[121]](#cite_note-123) later, Mao changed his mind and supported intervention to Hungary.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

In the 1–3 November 1956 period, Khrushchev informed the USSR's Warsaw Pact allies of his decision to repress the Hungarian Revolution. Khrushchev met with the Polish communist politician Władysław Gomułka in Brest, Belarus; and then spoke with the Romanian, Czechoslovak, and Bulgarian leaders in Bucharest, Romania. Finally, Khrushchev went to the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia and spoke with [Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito") who persuaded Khrushchev to install Kádár as the new leader of the People's Republic of Hungary, instead of [Ferenc Münnich](/wiki/Ferenc_M%C3%BCnnich "Ferenc Münnich").[[123]](#cite_note-125)[[124]](#cite_note-126) Two months after the USSR repressed the Hungarian Revolution, Tito told [Nikolay Firyubin](/wiki/Nikolay_Firyubin "Nikolay Firyubin"), the [Soviet ambassador to Yugoslavia](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_Russia_to_Yugoslavia "List of ambassadors of Russia to Yugoslavia"), that "(political) reaction raised its head, especially in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), where the [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") elements openly incited the employees of the Yugoslav security organs to violence."[[125]](#cite_note-127)

#### Polish response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Polish response")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Tablica_upami%C4%99tniaj%C4%85ca_solidarno%C5%9B%C4%87_polsko-w%C4%99giersk%C4%85_podczas_rewolucji_w%C4%99gierskiej_na_Krakowskim_Przedmie%C5%9Bciu_w_Warszawie.JPG/250px-Tablica_upami%C4%99tniaj%C4%85ca_solidarno%C5%9B%C4%87_polsko-w%C4%99giersk%C4%85_podczas_rewolucji_w%C4%99gierskiej_na_Krakowskim_Przedmie%C5%9Bciu_w_Warszawie.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tablica_upami%C4%99tniaj%C4%85ca_solidarno%C5%9B%C4%87_polsko-w%C4%99giersk%C4%85_podczas_rewolucji_w%C4%99gierskiej_na_Krakowskim_Przedmie%C5%9Bciu_w_Warszawie.JPG)

Plaque commemorating Polish-Hungarian solidarity during the Hungarian revolution of 1956, at [Krakowskie Przedmieście](/wiki/Krakowskie_Przedmie%C5%9Bcie "Krakowskie Przedmieście") Street 5, in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw").

The events in Hungary were met with a spontaneous reaction in Poland. Hungarian flags were displayed in many Polish towns and villages. After the Soviet invasion, the help given by the ordinary [Poles to Hungarians](/wiki/Pole_and_Hungarian_brothers_be "Pole and Hungarian brothers be") took on a considerable scale. Citizen organizations and self-acting aid committees were established throughout Poland to distribute aid to the Hungarian population, e.g. the Social Civic Committee of Creative Associations (Bydgoszcz), the Student Committee for Aid to Hungarians ([Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków")), the Society of Friends of Hungarians (Tarnów), the Committee to Aid the Hungarians (Lublin), and the Committee for Aid to Hungarians (Człuchów). In addition to the official support coordinated by the Polish Red Cross, one convoy was dispatched – one organized by the Student Aid Committee for Hungarians from Kraków. Other such initiatives were prevented.[[126]](#cite_note-128)

By 12 November, over 11,000 honorary blood donors had registered throughout Poland. [Polish Red Cross](/wiki/Polish_Red_Cross "Polish Red Cross") statistics show that by air transport alone (15 aircraft), 44 tonnes of medication, blood, and other medical supplies were delivered to Hungary. Assistance sent using road and rail transport was much higher. Polish aid is estimated at a value of approximately US$2 million in 1956 dollars.[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130)[[129]](#cite_note-131)

#### International perspective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: International perspective")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/1956_november_5._Eindhoven.jpg/250px-1956_november_5._Eindhoven.jpg)](/wiki/File:1956_november_5._Eindhoven.jpg)

Dutch anti-communists march in support of the Hungarian Revolution in [Eindhoven](/wiki/Eindhoven "Eindhoven"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") on 5 November 1956

On 24 October 1956, U.S. Secretary of State [John Foster Dulles](/wiki/John_Foster_Dulles "John Foster Dulles") (r. 1953–1959) recommended that the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") convene to discuss the USSR's invasion and occupation of the Hungarian People's Republic, without decisive result,[[130]](#cite_note-132) because, despite the [Protocol of Sèvres](/wiki/Protocol_of_S%C3%A8vres "Protocol of Sèvres") (22–24 October 1956), the Suez Crisis prevented the West from criticizing the imperialism of the USSR; the U.S. vice president [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") said that: "We couldn't, on one hand, complain about the Soviets intervening in Hungary, and, on the other hand, approve of the British and the French picking that particular time to intervene against [Gamal Abdel] [Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser")."[[131]](#cite_note-BorhiContRoll-133) Despite earlier demands for the [rollback](/wiki/Rollback "Rollback") of communism and of the [leftist](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") liberation of eastern Europe, Dulles told the USSR that: "We do not see these states [Hungary and Poland] as potential military allies."[[82]](#cite_note-IC-84)

On 4 November 1956, the USSR vetoed the Security Council's resolution criticising the USSR's invasion of Hungary. In its stead, [United Nations Security Council Resolution 120](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_120 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 120") was passed, which charged the General Assembly to meet to discuss the matter. Despite 50 votes for withdrawal, 8 votes against withdrawal, and 15 abstentions from the matter, the communist Kádár government of Hungary rejected the presence of UN observers in the Hungarian People's Republic.[[132]](#cite_note-134)

In the U.S., two facts determined the inaction of the Eisenhower government: (i) the U.S. Army study, *Hungary, Resistance Activities and Potentials* (January 1956), which recommended against U.S. military intervention to Hungary on the side of the Hungarian revolutionists;[[133]](#cite_note-135) and (ii) the secret warfare of the National Security Council that encouraged anti-communist political discontent in the Eastern Bloc only through [psychological warfare](/wiki/Psychological_warfare "Psychological warfare"), [sabotage](/wiki/Sabotage "Sabotage"), and [economic warfare](/wiki/Economic_warfare "Economic warfare").[[131]](#cite_note-BorhiContRoll-133)[[134]](#cite_note-136)

According to A. Ross Johnson in the *[International Journal of Intelligence and CounterIntelligence](/wiki/International_Journal_of_Intelligence_and_CounterIntelligence "International Journal of Intelligence and CounterIntelligence")*: "Contrary to allegations that continue to this day, RFE Hungarian broadcasts did not foment revolution, urge Hungarians to fight the Soviets, or promise Western assistance. They did criticize would-be reform leader Imre Nagy in personally vituperative terms and contained emotional bombast that Hungarian listeners could easily have interpreted as indicating Western solidarity and support."[[135]](#cite_note-137) After the USSR defeated the anti-communist Hungarian Revolution, the revolutionists criticised the CIA and its RFE network for having deceived the Hungarians into believing that the West—NATO and the US—would expel the USSR from the Hungarian People's Republic. Although incitements to violence were officially against RFE policy, an internal analysis by RFE adviser William Griffith found, as summarized by the [National Security Archive](/wiki/National_Security_Archive "National Security Archive") at [George Washington University](/wiki/George_Washington_University "George Washington University"), that "RFE broadcasts in several cases had implied that foreign aid would be forthcoming if the Hungarians succeeded in establishing a 'central military command'" and "appealed to the Hungarians to 'continue to fight vigorously'". Griffith himself wrote that "There were relatively few real policy violations"—specifically four out of more than 300 broadcasts under review—but conceded that "summaries often failed to reflect the content of the program as it was finally written (this is not the case only with programs where policy violation occurred; the summaries during the period under review in many other cases proved to be very inaccurate descriptions of the programs finally produced)."[[136]](#cite_note-138) László Borhi, writing in the *[Journal of Cold War Studies](/wiki/Journal_of_Cold_War_Studies "Journal of Cold War Studies")*, states that the RFE broadcasts sanctioned by CIA official [Cord Meyer](/wiki/Cord_Meyer "Cord Meyer") were often "incautious, even reckless" and undermined parallel efforts by the Eisenhower administration to negotiate Hungarian independence in a framework similar to [Finlandization](/wiki/Finlandization "Finlandization"): "These contradictory policies sabotaged the overall approach. The harder the insurgents fought, the less chance there was for a negotiated settlement. But the unwillingness of the United States to counter Soviet military action meant that the Hungarian quest for liberation was suicidal."[[131]](#cite_note-BorhiContRoll-133)

In 1998, the Hungarian ambassador [Géza Jeszenszky](/wiki/G%C3%A9za_Jeszenszky "Géza Jeszenszky") criticised Western inaction in 1956 as disingenuous, recalling that the political influence of the United Nations readily applied to resolving the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") (1950–1953).[[137]](#cite_note-cnn-139) Moreover, the study *Hungary, 1956: Reviving the Debate over U.S. (In)action During the Revolution* confirms that the Eisenhower government did not intervene to the Hungarian Revolution – which occurred in the Soviet sphere of influence – because the USSR would have responded with a nuclear war.[[138]](#cite_note-140)

## Soviet invasion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Soviet invasion")]

1 November newsreel about the situation in Hungary

On 1 November, Nagy received reports that Soviet forces had entered Hungary from the east and were moving towards Budapest.[[139]](#cite_note-141) Nagy sought and received assurances, which proved to be false, from [Soviet Ambassador](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_Russia_to_Hungary "List of ambassadors of Russia to Hungary") [Yuri Andropov](/wiki/Yuri_Andropov "Yuri Andropov") that the Soviet Union would not invade. The Cabinet, with Kádár in agreement, declared Hungary's neutrality, withdrew from the Warsaw Pact and requested assistance from the diplomatic corps in Budapest and [Dag Hammarskjöld](/wiki/Dag_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld "Dag Hammarskjöld"), UN Secretary-General, to defend Hungary's neutrality.[[140]](#cite_note-142) Andropov was asked to inform his government that Hungary would begin negotiations on the removal of Soviet forces immediately.[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144)

On 3 November, a Hungarian delegation led by Defence Minister Pál Maléter was invited to attend negotiations on Soviet withdrawal at the Soviet Military Command at [Tököl](/wiki/T%C3%B6k%C3%B6l "Tököl"), near Budapest. At around midnight that evening, General Ivan Serov, Chief of the Soviet Security Police ([KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB")) ordered the arrest of the Hungarian delegation,[[143]](#cite_note-145) and the next day, the Soviet army again attacked Budapest.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Fortepan_12830_R%C3%A1k%C3%B3czi_%C3%BAt_-_Ak%C3%A1cfa_utca_sarok._Ki%C3%A9gett_szovjet_BTR-152_p%C3%A1nc%C3%A9lozott_l%C3%B6v%C3%A9szsz%C3%A1ll%C3%ADt%C3%B3_j%C3%A1rm%C5%B1..jpg/250px-Fortepan_12830_R%C3%A1k%C3%B3czi_%C3%BAt_-_Ak%C3%A1cfa_utca_sarok._Ki%C3%A9gett_szovjet_BTR-152_p%C3%A1nc%C3%A9lozott_l%C3%B6v%C3%A9szsz%C3%A1ll%C3%ADt%C3%B3_j%C3%A1rm%C5%B1..jpg)](/wiki/File:Fortepan_12830_R%C3%A1k%C3%B3czi_%C3%BAt_-_Ak%C3%A1cfa_utca_sarok._Ki%C3%A9gett_szovjet_BTR-152_p%C3%A1nc%C3%A9lozott_l%C3%B6v%C3%A9szsz%C3%A1ll%C3%ADt%C3%B3_j%C3%A1rm%C5%B1..jpg)

A Soviet-built [BTR-152](/wiki/BTR-152 "BTR-152") armored personnel carrier burns on a Budapest street, November 1956.

The second Soviet intervention, codenamed "Operation Whirlwind", was launched by [Marshal](/wiki/Marshal_of_the_Soviet_Union "Marshal of the Soviet Union") [Ivan Konev](/wiki/Ivan_Konev "Ivan Konev").[[106]](#cite_note-presidium1nov-108)[[145]](#cite_note-troops-147) The [five Soviet divisions stationed in Hungary](/wiki/Southern_Group_of_Forces "Southern Group of Forces") before 23 October were reinforced; Soviet forces soon reached a total strength of 17 divisions.[[146]](#cite_note-148) The [8th Mechanized Army](/wiki/8th_Tank_Army "8th Tank Army") under command of Lieutenant General [Hamazasp Babadzhanian](/wiki/Hamazasp_Babadzhanian "Hamazasp Babadzhanian") and the 38th Army under Lieutenant General Hadzhi-Umar Mamsurov from the nearby [Carpathian Military District](/wiki/Carpathian_Military_District "Carpathian Military District") were deployed to Hungary for the operation.[[147]](#cite_note-149) Some rank-and-file Soviet soldiers reportedly believed they were being sent to [East Berlin](/wiki/East_Berlin "East Berlin") to fight German fascists.[[148]](#cite_note-Fryer-150) By 21:30 on 3 November, the Soviet Army had completely encircled Budapest.[[149]](#cite_note-UN_report_5C_p196-151)

At 03:00 on 4 November, Soviet tanks penetrated Budapest along the [Pest](/wiki/Pest,_Hungary "Pest, Hungary") side of the Danube in two thrusts: one up the Soroksári road from the south and the other down the [Váci road](/wiki/V%C3%A1ci_%C3%BAt "Váci út") from the north. Thus, before a single shot was fired, the Soviets had effectively split the city into two, controlled all bridgeheads and were shielded to the rear by the wide Danube River. Armoured units crossed into [Buda](/wiki/Buda "Buda") and, at 04:25, fired the first shots at the army barracks on Budaörsi Road. Soon, Soviet artillery and tank fire were heard in all of the districts of Budapest.[[149]](#cite_note-UN_report_5C_p196-151) Operation Whirlwind combined air strikes, artillery, and the co-ordinated tank–infantry action of 17 divisions.[[150]](#cite_note-152) The Soviet army deployed [T-34-85](/wiki/T-34 "T-34") [medium tanks](/wiki/Medium_tank "Medium tank") as well as the new [T-54s](/wiki/T-54/T-55 "T-54/T-55"), [heavy](/wiki/Heavy_tank "Heavy tank") [IS-3](/wiki/IS-3 "IS-3") tanks, 152mm [ISU-152](/wiki/ISU-152 "ISU-152") mobile [assault guns](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun") and open-top [BTR-152](/wiki/BTR-152 "BTR-152") armored personnel carriers.[[151]](#cite_note-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/J%C3%B3zsef_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_a_Corvin_%28Kisfaludy%29_k%C3%B6zn%C3%A9l._Harck%C3%A9ptelenn%C3%A9_tett_ISU-152-es_szovjet_rohaml%C3%B6vegek%2C_a_h%C3%A1tt%C3%A9rben_egy_T-34-85_harckocsi._Fortepan_24854.jpg/250px-J%C3%B3zsef_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_a_Corvin_%28Kisfaludy%29_k%C3%B6zn%C3%A9l._Harck%C3%A9ptelenn%C3%A9_tett_ISU-152-es_szovjet_rohaml%C3%B6vegek%2C_a_h%C3%A1tt%C3%A9rben_egy_T-34-85_harckocsi._Fortepan_24854.jpg)](/wiki/File:J%C3%B3zsef_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_a_Corvin_(Kisfaludy)_k%C3%B6zn%C3%A9l._Harck%C3%A9ptelenn%C3%A9_tett_ISU-152-es_szovjet_rohaml%C3%B6vegek,_a_h%C3%A1tt%C3%A9rben_egy_T-34-85_harckocsi._Fortepan_24854.jpg)

Two disabled Soviet [ISU-152](/wiki/ISU-152 "ISU-152") [assault guns](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun") in Budapest's [8th District](/wiki/J%C3%B3zsefv%C3%A1ros "Józsefváros") with an abandoned [T-34/85](/wiki/T-34 "T-34") tank in the background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Corvin_%28Kisfaludy%29_k%C3%B6z%2C_Corvin_mozi._Fortepan_24782.jpg/250px-Corvin_%28Kisfaludy%29_k%C3%B6z%2C_Corvin_mozi._Fortepan_24782.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corvin_(Kisfaludy)_k%C3%B6z,_Corvin_mozi._Fortepan_24782.jpg)

Corvin Cinema after the end of the revolution

Between 4 and 9 November, the Hungarian Army put up sporadic and disorganised resistance, with Zhukov reporting the disarming of twelve divisions, two armoured regiments and the entire Hungarian Air Force. Hungarian fighters continued their most formidable resistance in various districts of Budapest (most famously the [Battle of the Corvin Passage](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Corvin_Passage "Battle of the Corvin Passage")), in and around the city of Pécs in the [Mecsek Mountains](/wiki/Mecsek "Mecsek"), and in the industrial centre of [Dunaújváros](/wiki/Duna%C3%BAjv%C3%A1ros "Dunaújváros") (then called Sztálinváros). There were ten to fifteen thousand resistance fighters fighting in Budapest, with the heaviest fighting occurring in the working-class stronghold of Csepel on the Danube River.[[152]](#cite_note-Lindvai_2008-154)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Although some very senior officers were openly pro-Soviet, rank-and-file soldiers were overwhelmingly loyal to the revolution and either fought the invasion or deserted. The UN reported that there were no recorded incidents of Hungarian Army units fighting for the Soviets.[[153]](#cite_note-155)
