At 05:20 on 4 November, Nagy broadcast his final plea to the nation and the world, announcing that Soviet forces were attacking Budapest and that the government was remaining at its post.[[154]](#cite_note-156) The radio station, Free [Kossuth Rádió](/wiki/Kossuth_R%C3%A1di%C3%B3 "Kossuth Rádió"), stopped broadcasting at 08:07.[[155]](#cite_note-157) An emergency Cabinet meeting was held in the Parliament but was attended by only three ministers. As Soviet troops arrived to occupy the building, a negotiated evacuation ensued, leaving Minister of State [István Bibó](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Bib%C3%B3 "István Bibó") as the last representative of the National Government remaining at his post.[[156]](#cite_note-Bibo-158) He wrote *For Freedom and Truth*, a stirring proclamation to the nation and the world.[[157]](#cite_note-159)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Erzs%C3%A9bet_%28Lenin%29_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt%2C_a_Royal_Sz%C3%A1ll%C3%B3val_%28ma_Corinthia_Hotel%29_szemben._Fortepan_15317.jpg/250px-Erzs%C3%A9bet_%28Lenin%29_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt%2C_a_Royal_Sz%C3%A1ll%C3%B3val_%28ma_Corinthia_Hotel%29_szemben._Fortepan_15317.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erzs%C3%A9bet_(Lenin)_k%C3%B6r%C3%BAt,_a_Royal_Sz%C3%A1ll%C3%B3val_(ma_Corinthia_Hotel)_szemben._Fortepan_15317.jpg)

*Ruszkik haza!* ("Russians go home!") slogan in Budapest

At 06:00, 4 November,[[158]](#cite_note-160) in the town of [Szolnok](/wiki/Szolnok "Szolnok"), Kádár proclaimed the "Hungarian Revolutionary Worker-Peasant Government". His statement declared: "We must put an end to the excesses of the counter-revolutionary elements. The hour for action has sounded. We are going to defend the interest of the workers and peasants and the achievements of the people's democracy."[[159]](#cite_note-161)

Later that evening, Kádár called upon "the faithful fighters of the true cause of socialism" to come out of hiding and take up arms. Hungarian support did not materialise, and fighting did not take the form of a civil war, but rather, in the words of a United Nations report, that of "a well-equipped foreign army crushing by overwhelming force a national movement and eliminating the Government".[[160]](#cite_note-162)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/%C3%9Cll%C5%91i_%C3%BAt_a_Nagyk%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_fel%C5%91l_a_Vajdahunyad_utca_fel%C3%A9_n%C3%A9zve._Fortepan_24768.jpg/250px-%C3%9Cll%C5%91i_%C3%BAt_a_Nagyk%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_fel%C5%91l_a_Vajdahunyad_utca_fel%C3%A9_n%C3%A9zve._Fortepan_24768.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%9Cll%C5%91i_%C3%BAt_a_Nagyk%C3%B6r%C3%BAt_fel%C5%91l_a_Vajdahunyad_utca_fel%C3%A9_n%C3%A9zve._Fortepan_24768.jpg)

Rubble after the end of fighting in Budapest's 8th District

By 08:00, organised defence of the city evaporated after the radio station had been seized, and many defenders fell back to fortified positions.[[161]](#cite_note-UN_report_5C_p197-163) Between 08:00 and 09:00, the parliamentary guard laid down its arms, and forces under Major General [Kuzma Grebennik](/wiki/Kuzma_Grebennik "Kuzma Grebennik") captured Parliament and liberated captured ministers of the Rákosi–Hegedüs government. Among the liberated were [István Dobi](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Dobi "István Dobi") and [Sándor Rónai](/wiki/S%C3%A1ndor_R%C3%B3nai "Sándor Rónai"), both of whom became members of the re-established socialist Hungarian government.[[152]](#cite_note-Lindvai_2008-154) As Soviet troops also came under attack in civilian quarters, they were unable to differentiate military from civilian targets.[[162]](#cite_note-164) For that reason, Soviet tanks often crept along main roads and fired indiscriminately into buildings.[[161]](#cite_note-UN_report_5C_p197-163) Hungarian resistance was strongest in the industrial areas of Budapest, with Csepel heavily targeted by Soviet artillery and air strikes.[[163]](#cite_note-165)

The last holdouts against the Soviet assault were located in Csepel and in Dunaújváros, where fighting lasted until 11 November, before insurgents finally succumbed to the Soviets.[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] When the fighting ended, Hungarian casualties totalled approximately 2,500 dead and 20,000 wounded. Budapest bore the brunt of the bloodshed, with 1,569 civilians killed.[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Approximately 53% of the dead were workers; half of all Hungarian casualties were people younger than thirty. On the Soviet side, 699 men were killed, 1,450 wounded, and 51 [missing in action](/wiki/Missing_in_action "Missing in action"). An estimated 80% of all Soviet casualties occurred in the fighting against insurgents in the eighth and ninth districts of Budapest.[[66]](#cite_note-Lendvai_2008-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[164]](#cite_note-166)[[165]](#cite_note-167)

### Soviet perspective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Soviet perspective")]

Soviet reports of the events surrounding, during and after the disturbance were remarkably consistent in their accounts, more so after the Second Soviet intervention cemented support for the Soviet position among international communist parties. *[Pravda](/wiki/Pravda "Pravda")* published an account 36 hours after the outbreak of violence that set the tone for all further reports and subsequent Soviet historiography:[[166]](#cite_note-Barghoorn_SFP-168)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

1. On the afternoon of Tuesday, October 23, 1956, the "honest," socialist, Hungarian working people staged a demonstration against the anti-party mistakes committed by the Rakosi and Gero governments.
2. Fascist, Hitlerite, reactionary, counter-revolutionary hooligans, financed, trained, and equipped by the imperialist, capitalist west, took advantage of the demonstration to begin a counter-revolution.
3. The honest Hungarian working people under Imre Nagy appealed to Soviet forces stationed in Hungary under the Warsaw Pact for assistance in restoring order.
4. The Nagy government proved to be weak, and it permitted counter-revolutionary influences to penetrate its ranks. It became helpless and fell apart. This is demonstrated by the fact that Nagy denounced the Warsaw Pact.
5. The Hungarian patriots under János Kádár broke with the Nagy government and formed the Hungarian revolutionary workers and peasants government. This genuinely popular government asked the Soviet command for help in suppressing the counter-revolution.
6. The Hungarian patriots, assisted by Soviet forces, smashed the counter-revolution.

The first Soviet report came out 24 hours after the first Western report. Nagy's appeal to the United Nations was not reported. After Nagy was arrested outside the Yugoslav embassy, his arrest was not reported. Also, accounts failed to explain how Nagy went from patriot to traitor.[[167]](#cite_note-169) The Soviet press reported calm in Budapest, but the Western press reported a revolutionary crisis was breaking out. According to the Soviet account, Hungarians never wanted a revolution at all.[[166]](#cite_note-Barghoorn_SFP-168)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In January 1957, representatives of the Soviet Union, Bulgaria, Hungary, and Romania met in Budapest to review internal developments in Hungary since the establishment of the Soviet-imposed government. A communiqué on the meeting "unanimously concluded" that Hungarian workers, with the leadership of the Kádár government and the support of the Soviet army, defeated attempts "to eliminate the socialist achievements of the Hungarian people".[[168]](#cite_note-communique-170)

The Soviet, Chinese, and Warsaw Pact governments urged Kádár to proceed with the interrogation and trial of the ministers of the Nagy government, and asked for punitive measures against the "counter-revolutionists".[[168]](#cite_note-communique-170)[[169]](#cite_note-171) In addition, the Kádár government published an extensive series of "white books" (*The Counter-Revolutionary Forces in the October Events in Hungary*) that documented real incidents of violence against Communist Party and ÁVH members and the confessions of Nagy's supporters. The "white books" were widely distributed in several languages in most socialist countries and, while based in fact, they present factual evidence with a colouring and narrative that are not generally supported by non-Soviet-aligned historians.[[170]](#cite_note-172)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Budapest_339070207_1144456206223179_4357879065500815081_n.jpg/250px-Budapest_339070207_1144456206223179_4357879065500815081_n.jpg)](/wiki/File:Budapest_339070207_1144456206223179_4357879065500815081_n.jpg)

A monument commemorating those who died during the fighting on 25 October 1956

### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Hungary")]

In the immediate aftermath, many thousands of Hungarians were arrested. Eventually, 26,000 of these were brought before the Hungarian courts, 22,000 were sentenced and imprisoned, 13,000 were interned, and 229 were executed. Approximately 200,000[[171]](#cite_note-173) fled Hungary as refugees. Many resettled in Austria in the winter of 1956. These were helped by [CARE](/wiki/CARE "CARE"), an international relief organization. CARE provided refugees with welcome kits consisting of pajamas, knitting tools, school supplies, food, and water. The U.S. Department of Agriculture worked with CARE to use military vessels to transport over 500,000 packages to Vienna, where the Red Cross distributed supplies to refugees.[[172]](#cite_note-174)[[173]](#cite_note-Fink-175)[[174]](#cite_note-176)[[175]](#cite_note-Cseresneyes-177) Hungarian refugees of the conflict were resettled across 37 different countries, with most going to Austria.[[176]](#cite_note-178) Former Hungarian Foreign Minister Géza Jeszenszky estimated that 350 were executed.[[137]](#cite_note-cnn-139) Sporadic resistance and strikes by workers' councils continued until mid-1957, causing economic disruption.[[177]](#cite_note-179) By 1963, most [political prisoners](/wiki/Political_prisoner "Political prisoner") from the 1956 Hungarian Revolution had been released.[[178]](#cite_note-180)

With most of Budapest under Soviet control by 8 November, Kádár became Prime Minister of the "Revolutionary Worker-Peasant Government" and General Secretary of the Hungarian Communist Party. Few Hungarians rejoined the reorganised Party, its leadership having been purged under the supervision of the [Soviet Praesidium](/wiki/Presidium_of_the_Supreme_Soviet "Presidium of the Supreme Soviet"), led by [Georgy Malenkov](/wiki/Georgy_Malenkov "Georgy Malenkov") and Mikhail Suslov.[[179]](#cite_note-situation-181) Although Party membership declined from 800,000 before the uprising to 100,000 by December 1956, Kádár steadily increased his control over Hungary and neutralised dissenters. The new government attempted to enlist support by espousing popular principles of Hungarian self-determination voiced during the uprising, but Soviet troops remained.[[180]](#cite_note-182) After 1956 the Soviet Union severely purged the Hungarian Army and reinstituted political indoctrination in the units that remained. In May 1957, the Soviet Union increased its troop levels in Hungary and by treaty Hungary accepted the Soviet presence on a permanent basis.[[181]](#cite_note-183)

The [Red Cross](/wiki/International_Red_Cross_and_Red_Crescent_Movement "International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement") and the [Austrian Army](/wiki/Austrian_Armed_Forces "Austrian Armed Forces") established refugee camps in [Traiskirchen](/wiki/Traiskirchen "Traiskirchen") and [Graz](/wiki/Graz "Graz").[[175]](#cite_note-Cseresneyes-177)[[182]](#cite_note-184) Nagy along with [György Lukács](/wiki/Gy%C3%B6rgy_Luk%C3%A1cs "György Lukács"), [Géza Losonczy](/wiki/G%C3%A9za_Losonczy "Géza Losonczy"), and László Rajk's widow, Júlia, took refuge in the [Embassy of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Embassy_of_Serbia,_Budapest "Embassy of Serbia, Budapest") as Soviet forces overran Budapest. Despite assurances of safe passage out of Hungary by the Soviets and the Kádár government, Nagy and his group were arrested when attempting to leave the embassy on 22 November and taken to Romania. Losonczy died while on a hunger strike in prison awaiting trial when his jailers "carelessly pushed a feeding tube down his windpipe".[[183]](#cite_note-185)

The remainder of the group was returned to Budapest in 1958. Nagy was executed, along with Maléter and [Miklós Gimes](/wiki/Mikl%C3%B3s_Gimes "Miklós Gimes"), after secret trials in June 1958. Their bodies were placed in unmarked graves in the [Municipal Cemetery](/wiki/New_Public_Cemetery,_Budapest "New Public Cemetery, Budapest") outside Budapest.[[184]](#cite_note-BBCJune16-186)

During the November 1956 Soviet assault on Budapest, Cardinal Mindszenty was granted political asylum at the United States embassy, where he lived for the next 15 years, refusing to leave Hungary unless the government reversed his 1949 conviction for treason. Because of poor health and a request from the [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), he finally left the embassy for Austria in September 1971.[[185]](#cite_note-187)

Nicolas Krassó was one of the left leaders of the Hungarian uprising and member of the *[New Left Review](/wiki/New_Left_Review "New Left Review")* editorial committee. In an interview he gave to [Peter Gowan](/wiki/Peter_Gowan "Peter Gowan") shortly before his death, Krassó summed up the meaning of the Hungarian Revolution with a recollection from Stalin's short speech in the 19th Congress of the Soviet Union in 1952: "Stalin kept silent throughout the Congress till the very end when he made a short speech that covers about two and a half printed pages. He said there were two banners that the progressive bourgeoisie had thrown away and which the working class should pick up – the banners of democracy and national independence. Certainly nobody could doubt that in 1956 the Hungarian workers raised these banners high."[[186]](#cite_note-188)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### International

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: International")]

Despite Cold War rhetoric from western countries espousing rollback of the Soviet domination of eastern Europe, and Soviet promises of socialism's imminent triumph, national leaders of this period (as well as later historians) saw the failure of the Hungarian Revolution as evidence that the Cold War had come to a stalemate in Europe.[[187]](#cite_note-189)

In West Germany, Foreign Minister [Heinrich von Brentano](/wiki/Heinrich_von_Brentano "Heinrich von Brentano") recommended that the people of Eastern Europe be discouraged from "taking dramatic action which might have disastrous consequences for themselves". [The Secretary-General of NATO Paul-Henri Spaak](/wiki/Paul-Henri_Spaak "Paul-Henri Spaak") called the Hungarian revolt "the collective suicide of a whole people".[[188]](#cite_note-190) In a December 1956 meeting of the West German [Social-Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany"), Central Board member [Herbert Wehner](/wiki/Herbert_Wehner "Herbert Wehner") condemned what he termed the uprising's "fever of destruction against everybody believed to be members or officials of the Communist Party."[[189]](#cite_note-191) In a newspaper interview in 1957, Khrushchev commented, "support by United States ... is rather in the nature of the support that the rope gives to a hanged man".[[190]](#cite_note-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Eleanor_Roosevelt_and_the_Hungarian_Freedom_Fighters_at_Camp_Roeder_in_Salzburg%2C_Austria_-_NARA_-_195987.jpg/250px-Eleanor_Roosevelt_and_the_Hungarian_Freedom_Fighters_at_Camp_Roeder_in_Salzburg%2C_Austria_-_NARA_-_195987.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eleanor_Roosevelt_and_the_Hungarian_Freedom_Fighters_at_Camp_Roeder_in_Salzburg,_Austria_-_NARA_-_195987.jpg)

[Eleanor Roosevelt](/wiki/Eleanor_Roosevelt "Eleanor Roosevelt") meets exiled Hungarian revolutionaries at Camp Roeder in Salzburg, 10 May 1957

In January 1957, United Nations Secretary-General Dag Hammarskjöld, acting in response to UN General Assembly resolutions requesting investigation and observation of the events in Soviet-occupied Hungary, established the Special Committee on the Problem of Hungary.[[191]](#cite_note-193) The committee, with representatives from [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), Ceylon (now [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka")), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia") and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay"), conducted hearings in New York, Geneva, Rome, Vienna and London. Over five months, 111 refugees were interviewed including ministers, military commanders and other officials of the Nagy government, workers, revolutionary council members, factory managers and technicians, communists and non-communists, students, writers, teachers, medical personnel, and Hungarian soldiers. Documents, newspapers, radio transcripts, photos, film footage, and other records from Hungary were also reviewed, as well as written testimony of 200 other Hungarians.[[192]](#cite_note-194)

The governments of Hungary and Romania refused entry to the officials of this committee, and the government of the Soviet Union did not respond to requests for information.[[193]](#cite_note-195) The 268-page Committee Report[[194]](#cite_note-196) was presented to the General Assembly in June 1957, documenting the course of the uprising and Soviet intervention and concluding that "the Kádár government and Soviet occupation were in violation of the human rights of the Hungarian people".[[195]](#cite_note-197) A General Assembly resolution was approved, deploring "the repression of the Hungarian people and the Soviet occupation", but no other action was taken.[[196]](#cite_note-198)

The chairman of the Special Committee was [Alsing Andersen](/wiki/Alsing_Andersen "Alsing Andersen"), a Danish politician and leading figure of Denmark's Social Democratic Party, who had served in the Buhl government in 1942 during the Nazi German occupation of Denmark. He had defended collaboration with the occupation forces and denounced the resistance.[[197]](#cite_note-199)[[198]](#cite_note-200) The Committee Report and the motives of its authors were criticised by the delegations to the United Nations from the Soviet Union and Kádár government. The Hungarian representative disagreed with the report's conclusions, accusing it of falsifying the events, and argued that the establishment of the committee was illegal. The committee was accused of being hostile to Hungary and its social system.[[199]](#cite_note-201) An article in the Soviet journal *International Affairs*, published by the Foreign Affairs Ministry, carried an article in 1957 in which it denounced the report as a "collection of falsehoods and distortions".[[200]](#cite_note-202)

*[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* named the Hungarian Freedom Fighter its [Man of the Year](/wiki/Time_Person_of_the_Year "Time Person of the Year") for 1956. The accompanying *Time* article comments that this choice could not have been anticipated until the explosive events of the revolution, almost at the end of 1956. The magazine cover and accompanying text displayed an artist's depiction of a Hungarian freedom fighter, and used pseudonyms for the three participants whose stories are the subject of the article.[[201]](#cite_note-203) In 2006, Hungarian Prime Minister Ferenc Gyurcsány referred to this famous *Time* cover as "the faces of free Hungary" in a speech marking the 50th anniversary of the uprising.[[202]](#cite_note-204) Mr Gyurcsány (in a joint appearance with British Prime Minister [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair")) commented "It is an idealised image but the faces of the figures are really the face of the revolutionaries".[[203]](#cite_note-205)

At the [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") in Melbourne, the Soviet handling of the Hungarian uprising led to a boycott by Spain, the Netherlands, and Switzerland.[[204]](#cite_note-206) A confrontation between Soviet and Hungarian teams occurred in the [semi-final match](/wiki/Water_polo_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics "Water polo at the 1956 Summer Olympics") of the [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo") tournament on 6 December. The match was extremely violent, and was halted in the final minute to quell fighting among spectators. This match, now known as the "[Blood in the Water match](/wiki/Blood_in_the_Water_match "Blood in the Water match")", became the subject of several films.[[205]](#cite_note-207)[[206]](#cite_note-208) The Hungarian team won the game 4–0 and later was awarded the Olympic gold medal. [Norway](/wiki/Norway_national_bandy_team "Norway national bandy team") declined an invitation to the inaugural [Bandy World Championship](/wiki/1957_Bandy_World_Championship "1957 Bandy World Championship") in 1957, citing the presence of a team from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_bandy_team "Soviet Union national bandy team") as the reason.

On Sunday, January 6, 1957, as millions of Americans watched [Ed Sullivan](/wiki/Ed_Sullivan "Ed Sullivan")'s popular television [variety show](/wiki/Variety_show "Variety show"), with the nearly 22 year-old [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") headlining for the third time, Sullivan told viewers Presley felt "so keenly about Hungarian relief, he urges all of us through the country to remember that immediate aid is needed." The host followed this with Elvis singing an unrecorded number, the gospel song *[Peace in the Valley](/wiki/Peace_in_the_Valley "Peace in the Valley")*, saying "he feels that this is sort of in the mood that he'd like to create." By the end of 1957, these contributions, distributed by the Geneva-based [International Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross") as food rations, clothing and other essentials, had amounted to some [CHF](/wiki/Swiss_franc "Swiss franc") 26 million (US$6 million in 1957 dollars), the equivalent of $68,800,000 in today's dollars.[[207]](#cite_note-inflation-US-209) On 1 March 2011, [István Tarlós](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Tarl%C3%B3s "István Tarlós"), the [mayor of Budapest](/wiki/Mayor_of_Budapest "Mayor of Budapest"), made Presley an honorary citizen posthumously, and a plaza located at the intersection of two of the city's most important avenues was named after Presley as a gesture of gratitude.

Meanwhile, as the 1950s drew to a close the events in Hungary produced fractures within the Communist political parties of Western European countries. In San Marino, some moderate Socialist members of a Socialist-Communist coalition government broke their alliance with the Communists, who failed to condemn the Soviet Union's repression of the Hungarians. This schism resulted in a perfect 30–30 split in the council that paralyzed the government and led to the [fatti di Rovereta](/wiki/Fatti_di_Rovereta "Fatti di Rovereta"), a constitutional crisis which resulted in the victory of a new democratic government and brought the twelve years of communist rule in San Marino to an end.[[208]](#cite_note-210) The [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI) suffered a split. According to the official newspaper of the PCI, *[l'Unità](/wiki/L%27Unit%C3%A0 "L'Unità")*, most ordinary members and the Party leadership, including [Palmiro Togliatti](/wiki/Palmiro_Togliatti "Palmiro Togliatti") and [Giorgio Napolitano](/wiki/Giorgio_Napolitano "Giorgio Napolitano"), supported the actions of the Soviet Union in suppressing the uprising.[[209]](#cite_note-211) However, [Giuseppe Di Vittorio](/wiki/Giuseppe_Di_Vittorio "Giuseppe Di Vittorio"), chief of the communist trade union [CGIL](/wiki/Italian_General_Confederation_of_Labour "Italian General Confederation of Labour"), spoke out against the leadership's position, as did prominent party members [Antonio Giolitti](/wiki/Antonio_Giolitti "Antonio Giolitti"), [Loris Fortuna](/wiki/Loris_Fortuna "Loris Fortuna"), and many others influential in the Communist Party. [Pietro Nenni](/wiki/Pietro_Nenni "Pietro Nenni") of the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party"), a close ally of the PCI, opposed the Soviet intervention as well. Napolitano, elected in 2006 as [President of the Italian Republic](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy"), wrote in his 2005 political autobiography that he regretted his justification of Soviet action in Hungary, stating at the time he believed that party unity and the leadership of Soviet Communism was more important.[[210]](#cite_note-212)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The [Communist Party of Great Britain](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Great_Britain "Communist Party of Great Britain") (CPGB) suffered the loss of thousands of party members following the events in Hungary. Though [Peter Fryer](/wiki/Peter_Fryer "Peter Fryer"), correspondent for the CPGB newspaper *[The Daily Worker](/wiki/Morning_Star_(British_newspaper) "Morning Star (British newspaper)")*, reported on the violent suppression of the uprising, his dispatches were heavily censored by the party leadership.[[211]](#cite_note-213)[[148]](#cite_note-Fryer-150) Upon his return from Hungary Fryer resigned from the paper. He was later expelled by the Communist Party.

In France, moderate communists, such as historian [Emmanuel Le Roy Ladurie](/wiki/Emmanuel_Le_Roy_Ladurie "Emmanuel Le Roy Ladurie"), resigned, questioning the [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party")'s policy of supporting Soviet actions. The French philosopher and writer [Albert Camus](/wiki/Albert_Camus "Albert Camus") wrote an [open letter](/wiki/Open_letter "Open letter"), *The Blood of the Hungarians*, criticising the West's lack of action. Even [Jean-Paul Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre"), still a determined Communist, criticised the Soviets in his article *Le Fantôme de Staline*, in *Situations VII*.[[212]](#cite_note-214) [Left communists](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism")[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] were particularly supportive of the revolution.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Chinese Communist Party (CCP) viewed the events of the revolution in context of its concern with the Soviet de-Stalinization of 1956 and the sort of problems along the lines of the Eastern European uprisings that China might eventually have to face.[[213]](#cite_note-:0-215) Some Chinese leaders viewed the events, together with the protests in Poland, as demonstrating the dangers of overemphasizing development of heavy industry while underemphasizing people's livelihoods.[[214]](#cite_note-:02-216) Mao cited the repressions in Hungary as a lesson: "You forbid people to strike, to petition, or to make unfavorable comments, you simply resort to repression in every case, until one day you become a Rákosi."[[213]](#cite_note-:0-215) Later, as the CCP leadership became concerned with the degree of criticism from the lower levels of the party and from outside of the party during the [Hundred Flowers campaign](/wiki/Hundred_Flowers_Campaign "Hundred Flowers Campaign"), Mao again cited the Hungarian revolution, describing China as at risk of a "Hungarian Incident" if it did not win this "major battle".[[215]](#cite_note-217) In contrast, the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT) [UN representative](/wiki/Permanent_Representative_of_China_to_the_United_Nations "Permanent Representative of China to the United Nations") of [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei")-based Republic of China [Tsiang Tingfu](/wiki/Tsiang_Tingfu "Tsiang Tingfu") condemned Soviet actions in Hungary following the crackdown while the [Chinese government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Republic_of_China "Government of the Republic of China") collected contributions towards the relief of Hungarian refugees.[[216]](#cite_note-218)

### Commemoration and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Commemoration and legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Budape%C5%A1%C5%A5%2C_H%C3%B6s%C3%B6k_t%C3%A9re%2C_pam%C4%9Btn%C3%AD_deska_1956.jpg/250px-Budape%C5%A1%C5%A5%2C_H%C3%B6s%C3%B6k_t%C3%A9re%2C_pam%C4%9Btn%C3%AD_deska_1956.jpg)](/wiki/File:Budape%C5%A1%C5%A5,_H%C3%B6s%C3%B6k_t%C3%A9re,_pam%C4%9Btn%C3%AD_deska_1956.jpg)

Memorial plaque at the [Embassy of Serbia, Budapest](/wiki/Embassy_of_Serbia,_Budapest "Embassy of Serbia, Budapest") in memory of [Imre Nagy](/wiki/Imre_Nagy "Imre Nagy"), who took sanctuary in what was the Yugoslav embassy during the Hungarian Revolution.

In the north-west corner of [MacArthur Park](/wiki/MacArthur_Park "MacArthur Park") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), the Hungarian-American community built a commemorative statue to honour the Hungarian freedom fighters. Built in the late 1960s, the obelisk statue stands with an American eagle watching over the city of Los Angeles. There are several monuments dedicated to the Commemoration of the Hungarian Revolution throughout the United States. One such monument may be found in Cleveland, Ohio, at the Cardinal Mindszenty Plaza. There is also a monument of "A Boy From Pest" in the town of [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin "Szczecin"), Poland. [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver") has Hungarian Freedom Park, named in 1968 to commemorate the uprising.[[217]](#cite_note-219)

Public discussion about the revolution was suppressed in Hungary for more than 30 years. Since the thaw of the 1980s, it has been a subject of intense study and debate. At the inauguration of the Third Hungarian Republic [in 1989](/wiki/End_of_communism_in_Hungary "End of communism in Hungary"), 23 October was declared a [national holiday](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Hungary "Public holidays in Hungary").

On 16 June 1989, at the 31st commemoration of his execution, Nagy's body was reburied with full honours.[[184]](#cite_note-BBCJune16-186) The Republic of Hungary was declared in 1989 on the 33rd anniversary of the Revolution, and 23 October is now a Hungarian national holiday.[[218]](#cite_note-220)

In December 1991, the preamble of the treaties signed with the dismembered Soviet Union, under [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev"), and Russia, represented by [Boris Yeltsin](/wiki/Boris_Yeltsin "Boris Yeltsin"), officially apologised for the Soviet actions of 1956 in Hungary. Yeltsin made the apology in person during a 1992 speech to the Hungarian Parliament.[[137]](#cite_note-cnn-139)

In 2000, the Hungarian government passed "Ilona Tóth's law", absolving those that were convicted for their involvement with the revolution, inspired by the case of [Ilona Tóth](/wiki/Ilona_T%C3%B3th "Ilona Tóth").[[219]](#cite_note-:1-221)

On 13 February 2006, the [U.S. State Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") commemorated the fiftieth anniversary of the 1956 Hungarian Revolution. U.S. Secretary of State [Condoleezza Rice](/wiki/Condoleezza_Rice "Condoleezza Rice") commented on the contributions made by 1956 Hungarian refugees to the United States and other host countries, as well as the [role of Hungary](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") in providing refuge to East Germans during the 1989 protests against communist rule.[[220]](#cite_note-222) U.S. President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") also visited Hungary on 22 June 2006 to commemorate the fiftieth anniversary.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

In August 2023, a history textbook written by former Minister of Culture of Russia [Vladimir Medinsky](/wiki/Vladimir_Medinsky "Vladimir Medinsky"), claimed that the 1956 Hungarian Revolution was a fascist uprising organised by the West.[[222]](#cite_note-224) On 25 September 2023, Hungary's Minister of Foreign Affairs and Trade [Péter Szijjártó](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_Szijj%C3%A1rt%C3%B3 "Péter Szijjártó") said "labelling these people as fascists is simply unacceptable".[[223]](#cite_note-225)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The UN General Assembly *Special Committee on the Problem of Hungary* (1957) estimated 75,000–200,000 soldiers and 1,600–4,000 tanks [OSZK.hu](http://mek.oszk.hu/01200/01274/01274.pdf) (p. 56). Soviet archives (available in [Lib.ru](/wiki/Lib.ru "Lib.ru"), Maksim Moshkow's Library) list 31,550 soldiers, 1,130 tanks, and [self-propelled artillery](/wiki/Self-propelled_artillery "Self-propelled artillery") pieces. [Lib.ru](http://fan.lib.ru/c/chekmarew_w_a/text_0200.shtml) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20100209184727/http://fan.lib.ru/c/chekmarew_w_a/text_0200.shtml) 9 February 2010 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") (in Russian).
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Alternate names for the revolution are *Hungarian Uprising* and *Hungarian Revolt*; the first term used the word *felkelés* ("uprising"); in the Communist period of 1957–1988, the term used was *Ellenforradalom* (Counter-revolution); and, since 1990, the official term for the Hungarian Revolution is the phrase: *Forradalom és szabadságharc* (Revolution and the Fight for Freedom), which evokes the [Hungarian Revolution of 1848](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1848 "Hungarian Revolution of 1848"). Linguistically, whereas the English *revolution* corresponds to the Hungarian *forradalom* [(U.S. State Dept. background on Hungary)](https://2009-2017.state.gov/r/pa/ei/bgn/26566.htm), the Oxford English Dictionary distinguishes between a *revolution*, which deposes a government, and an armed *revolt*, which might fail or succeed.

"in Hungarian: Magyarok! Nagy Imre miniszterelnök a ma hajnali szovjet támadáskor a szovjet követségre ment a tárgyalások folytatására, és onnan visszatérni már nem tudott. A reggel összehívott minisztertanácson a Parlament épületében tartózkodó Tildy Zoltánon kívül már csak B. Szabó István és Bibó István államminiszter tudott megérkezni. Mikor a Parlamentet a szovjet csapatok körülfogták, Tildy államminiszter a vérontás elkerülése végett megállapodást kötött velük, mely szerint ők megszállják az épületet, a benne levő polgári személyek pedig szabadon távozhatnak. Ő, a megállapodáshoz tartva magát, eltávozott. Az országgyűlés épületében egyedül alulírott Bibó István államminiszter maradtam, mint az egyedüli törvényes magyar kormány egyedüli képviselője. Ebben a helyzetben a következőket nyilatkozom: In English: To My Fellow Hungarians! When the Soviet Army attacked today at dawn, Prime Minister Nagy Imre went to the Soviet Embassy to negotiate and could not return. Tildy Zoltán, who was already in the Parliament building, and ministers Szabó István and Bibó István attended the council of ministers meeting that was convened this morning. As Soviet troops surrounded the Parliament building, minister Tildy Zoltán, to avoid bloodshed, reached an agreement, by which Soviet soldiers would occupy the Parliament building and allow all civilians to evacuate. According to this agreement, he then departed. Only the undersigned, Bibó István, remained in the Parliament building as the only representative of the only existing legal Hungarian government. Under these circumstances, I make the following declaration: ([Available in English](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/For_Freedom_and_Truth "s:For Freedom and Truth"))"

### Feature films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Feature films")]

* *[The Beast of Budapest](/wiki/The_Beast_of_Budapest "The Beast of Budapest")*, a 1958 American film
* [*Freedom's Fury*](http://www.rferl.org/featuresarticle/2006/05/0e5164bb-fd53-4562-88c5-9aac69e5845c.html)—The 2005 documentary film depicting events surrounding the Hungarian–Soviet confrontation in the Olympic water polo tournament, now known as the "blood in the water match". Narrated by Mark Spitz, produced by Lucy Liu and Quentin Tarantino.
* [*Torn from the Flag*](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0468559/)—Documentary film 2007. The significant global effects of the Hungarian revolution of 1956.
* [*Freedom Dance*](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt1042885/)—Multi award-winning animated documentary produced by [Steven Thomas Fischer](/wiki/Steven_Thomas_Fischer "Steven Thomas Fischer") and Craig Herron. The film retells the escape of Edward and Judy Hilbert from Communist Hungary during the Hungarian Revolution of 1956. The film is narrated by Golden Globe-winning actress [Mariska Hargitay](/wiki/Mariska_Hargitay "Mariska Hargitay").
* *[The Unburied Man](/wiki/The_Unburied_Man "The Unburied Man")*—Drama film on the life of Imre Nagy.
* [*The Forgotten Faces*](https://m.imdb.com/title/tt0317522/)—An amateur short film which retells the Hungarian revolution of 1956. Written and directed by [Peter Watkins](/wiki/Peter_Watkins "Peter Watkins") and filmed on the streets of [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury "Canterbury"), England.

### Commemorations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Commemorations")]

* [The 1956 Portal](https://web.archive.org/web/20091030033237/http://www.hungary1956.com/) – A resource for Hungarian-American organizations to highlight and promote their 1956 Hungarian Revolution commemoration activities, including 1956 photos, videos, resources, and events across the United States
* [Freedom Fighter 56](http://www.freedomfighter56.com/en_stories_2all.html) – Personal stories of survival and escape from participants in the revolution
* [1956 Hungarian Memorial Oral History Project](https://web.archive.org/web/20080915210152/http://www.multiculturalcanada.ca/node/1521) – Multicultural Canada oral history collection of revolution refugees in Canada
* [From the noon bell to the lads of Pest](http://mek.oszk.hu/09400/09426/index.phtml)
